Motivation: Traditional continuous inference loops in web agents are expensive and inefficient due to their linear growth in token expenditure and API latency as tasks repeat, making scaling difficult.

Method: This approach decouples reasoning from execution by using a one-shot LLM invocation to generate a JSON workflow blueprint from sanitized DOM data, which a lightweight runtime then executes. This reduces inference scaling complexity to amortized O(1).

Result: Empirical evaluation shows zero-shot compilation success rates ranging from 80-94%, with nearly 100% reliability achieved via minimal human intervention. Cost reductions to as low as $0.002 per compilation further validate the approach.

Conclusion: The proposed architecture demonstrates that deterministic compilation can enable cost-effective, scalable automation, overcoming the limitations of continuous inference systems.

Abstract: LLM-driven web agents operating through continuous inference loops -- repeatedly querying a model to evaluate browser state and select actions -- exhibit a fundamental scalability constraint for repetitive tasks. We characterize this as the Rerun Crisis: the linear growth of token expenditure and API latency relative to execution frequency. For a 5-step workflow over 500 iterations, a continuous agent incurs approximately 150.00 USD in inference costs; even with aggressive caching, this remains near 15.00 USD. We propose a Compile-and-Execute architecture that decouples LLM reasoning from browser execution, reducing per-workflow inference cost to under 0.10 USD. A one-shot LLM invocation processes a token-efficient semantic representation from a DOM Sanitization Module (DSM) and emits a deterministic JSON workflow blueprint. A lightweight runtime then drives the browser without further model queries. We formalize this cost reduction from O(M x N) to amortized O(1) inference scaling, where M is the number of reruns and N is the sequential actions. Empirical evaluation across data extraction, form filling, and fingerprinting tasks yields zero-shot compilation success rates of 80-94%. Crucially, the modularity of the JSON intermediate representation allows minimal Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) patching to elevate execution reliability to near-100%. At per-compilation costs between 0.002 USD and 0.092 USD across five frontier models, these results establish deterministic compilation as a paradigm enabling economically viable automation at scales previously infeasible under continuous architectures.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [265] [Monitoring Neural Training with Topology: A Footprint-Predictable Collapse Index](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26984)
*Alexander Kalinowski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method to monitor neural representations for signs of representational collapse using Modular Morse Homology Maintenance (MMHM) and a Collapse Index (CI).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To detect and mitigate representational collapse in neural networks, which can harm downstream performance before it is reflected in metrics.

Method: An online and topology-aware monitoring approach based on MMHM and CI, utilizing sparse updates and discrete Morse matching to track changes in neural representations efficiently.

Result: The proposed Collapse Index (CI) provides a low-latency early-warning signal, tested during LLM fine-tuning and KGE training, enabling in-training interventions.

Conclusion: The monitoring method allows for efficient detection of representational collapse, with potential for application across various neural network training scenarios.

Abstract: Representational collapse, where embeddings become anisotropic and lose multi-scale structure, can erode downstream performance long before performance metrics react. We propose an online, topology-aware monitor for evolving neural representations that couples Modular Morse Homology Maintenance (MMHM) with a composite Collapse Index (CI). Instead of rebuilding complexes each epoch, we apply sparse edits at a fixed scale and maintain a discrete Morse matching, yielding fast, incremental updates. Across LLM fine-tuning and temporal KGE training, CI provides a low-latency early-warning signal suitable for in-training interventions. Code and experimental scripts will be released publicly

</details>


### [266] [AutoSP: Unlocking Long-Context LLM Training Via Compiler-Based Sequence Parallelism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27089)
*Ahan Gupta,Zhihao Wang,Neel Dani,Masahiro Tanaka,Olatunji Ruwase,Minjia Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces AutoSP, an automated system to optimize Large Language Models (LLMs) for long-context training, enhancing trainability without significant runtime cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing libraries focus on optimizing LLM models with large parameters, neglecting specialized solutions for long-context training which involves complex and time-consuming manual setup.

Method: AutoSP integrates automated sequence parallelism and long-context aware activation-checkpointing into training pipelines, leveraging a compilation-based approach.

Result: AutoSP improved training context lengths by up to 2.7x on NVIDIA hardware and 2.5x on AMD hardware, offering enhancements at negligible runtime performance cost.

Conclusion: AutoSP simplifies and automates long-context optimizations for LLM training, making such processes accessible and efficient for developers.

Abstract: Large-language-models (LLMs) demonstrate enormous utility in long-context tasks which require processing prompts that consist of tens to hundreds of thousands of tokens. However, existing LLM training libraries do not provide easy to use abstractions to optimize for long-context training, instead focusing on optimizations for models with large parameter counts through ZeRO-3/FSDP, Tensor and Pipeline parallelism. This forces users to rewrite LLM training libraries to incorporate compositions of various complex long-context optimizations, such as sequence-parallelism, to training pipelines; a process that requires in-depth expertise, reducing developer productivity. To tackle these challenges, we introduce AutoSP: the first automated solution to automatically optimize LLM training for longer-contexts. AutoSP compiles models and applies a targeted set of optimizations: automated sequence parallelism, and long-context aware activation-checkpointing, to drastically enhance LLM trainability at negligible cost to throughput. Our evaluation demonstrates AutoSP's capability on both NVIDIA and AMD hardware, increasing training contexts by upto 2.7$\times$ and 2.5$\times$ respectively over competitive hand-written baseline at negligible cost to runtime performance.

</details>


### [267] [Simple Self-Conditioning Adaptation for Masked Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26985)
*Michael Cardei,Huu Binh Ta,Ferdinando Fioretto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Self-Conditioned Masked Diffusion Models (SCMDM), a method that improves masked diffusion models by conditioning on their own previous outputs, achieving better performance across various domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Masked Diffusion Models face limitations in cross-step refinement due to discarding clean-state predictions when tokens remain masked after updates.

Method: The proposed SCMDM method conditions each denoising step on the model's prior clean-state predictions without requiring architectural changes, extra computation, or auxiliary models.

Result: SCMDM achieves significant improvements across tasks, such as a 50% decrease in generative perplexity in a language model, as well as advancements in image synthesis, molecular generation, and genomic modeling.

Conclusion: SCMDM demonstrates the effectiveness of self-conditioning in diffusion models, highlighting its superiority over traditional approaches like partial self-conditioning and dropout strategies.

Abstract: Masked diffusion models (MDMs) generate discrete sequences by iterative denoising under an absorbing masking process. In standard masked diffusion, if a token remains masked after a reverse update, the model discards its clean-state prediction for that position. Thus, still-masked positions must be repeatedly inferred from the mask token alone. This design choice limits cross-step refinement. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a simple, yet effective, post-training adaptation for MDMs that conditions each denoising step on the model's own previous clean-state predictions. The resulting method, called Self-Conditioned Masked Diffusion Models (SCMDM), requires minimal architectural change, does not introduce a recurrent latent-state pathway, does not rely on an auxiliary reference model, and adds no extra denoiser evaluations during sampling. This is an important departure from partial self-conditioning approaches which requires expensive model training from scratch. In particular, the paper shows that partial self-conditioning, including the commonly used 50% dropout strategy for training self-conditioned models from scratch, is suboptimal in the post-training regime. Instead, once the model's self-generated clean-state estimates become informative, the specialization to refinement is preferable to mixing conditional and unconditional objectives. SCMDM is evaluated across multiple domains, demonstrating consistent improvement over vanilla MDM baselines, achieving nearly a 50% reduction in generative perplexity on OWT-trained models (42.89 to 23.72), alongside strong improvements in discretized image synthesis quality, small molecular generation, and enhanced fidelity in genomic distribution modeling.

</details>


### [268] [People-Centred Medical Image Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.26991)
*Zheng Zhang,Milad Masroor,Cuong Nguyen,Tahir Hassan,Yuanhong Chen,David Rosewarne,Kevin Wells,Thanh-Toan Do,Gustavo Carneiro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a framework, PecMan, to enhance medical AI by optimizing fairness, accuracy, and workflow integration, addressing clinical adoption barriers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limited clinical adoption of high-performing medical AI systems arises from fairness biases and ineffective workflow integration, hindering collaboration and trust.

Method: Proposed the PecMan framework with a dynamic gating mechanism and introduced the FairHAI benchmark to optimize and evaluate fairness, accuracy, and workload constraints.

Result: PecMan showed superior performance compared to previous methods in balancing fairness, accuracy, and clinician workload, based on experimental results.

Conclusion: Integrating fairness, diagnostic accuracy, and workflow considerations enables more clinically reliable and adoptable AI systems like PecMan.

Abstract: Recent advances in data-centric medical AI have produced highly accurate diagnostic systems, but the emphasis on data curation and performance metrics has not translated into widespread clinical adoption. We conjecture that this limited uptake stems from insufficient attention dedicated to the optimisation of fair performance across diverse patient populations and to workflow integration: performance biases can create regulatory barriers, and poorly integrated automation can disrupt clinical routines, degrade the quality of human-AI collaboration, and reduce clinicians' willingness to adopt AI tools. Prior work on workflow integration (e.g., Learning to Defer (L2D) and Learning to Complement (L2C)) and AI fairness has typically examined these challenges in isolation, overlooking their natural interdependence and the practical constraints of clinical environments, such as restricted clinician availability. We propose People-Centred Medical Image Analysis (PecMan), a human-AI framework that jointly optimises fairness, diagnostic accuracy, and workflow effectiveness through a dynamic gating mechanism that assigns cases to AI, clinicians, or both under clinician workload constraints. We also introduce the Fairness and Human-Centred AI (FairHAI) benchmark for evaluating trade-offs between accuracy, fairness, and clinician workload. Experiments using this benchmark show that PecMan consistently outperforms existing methods, paving the way for more trustworthy and clinically viable AI systems. Code will be available upon paper acceptance.

</details>


### [269] [When Continual Learning Moves to Memory: A Study of Experience Reuse in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27003)
*Qisheng Hu,Quanyu Long,Wenya Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Memory-augmented LLM agents avoid parameter updates by using external memory, but continual learning still presents challenges during memory retrieval under limited context. The study introduces a (k,v) external memory framework to analyze this problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether memory-augmented LLM agents truly resolve challenges in continual learning or simply shift them from model parameters to external memory.

Method: The study proposes a (k,v) framework for analyzing external memory, conducting sequential-task experiments in ALFWorld and BabyAI to evaluate memory representation and retrieval performance.

Result: Abstract procedural memories showed reliable transfer, while negative transfer disproportionately affected difficult cases. Furthermore, finer memory organization benefited forward transfer but increased forgetting.

Conclusion: External memory does not eliminate continual-learning problems but changes them to issues of memory representation and retrieval design.

Abstract: Memory-augmented LLM agents offer an appealing shortcut to continual learning: rather than updating model parameters, they accumulate experience in external memory, seemingly sidestepping the stability-plasticity dilemma of parametric learning. We show that this challenge does not disappear but resurfaces at the memory level. Under a limited context window, old and new experiences compete during retrieval, relocating the continual-learning bottleneck from parameter updates to memory access. To study this phenomenon, we introduce a (k,v) framework that disentangles two fundamental design axes of external memory: how experience is represented and how it is organized for retrieval. Across sequential-task experiments in ALFWorld and BabyAI, we find that abstract procedural memories transfer more reliably than detailed trajectories, while negative transfer disproportionately harms the hard cases. Moreover, finer-grained memory organization is not universally beneficial: designs that yield strong forward transfer can simultaneously induce severe forgetting. Together, these results reveal that external memory does not resolve the continual-learning problem; it reshapes it into a problem of memory representation and retrieval design.

</details>


### [270] [Automatic Causal Fairness Analysis with LLM-Generated Reporting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27011)
*Alessia Berarducci,Eric Rossetto,Alessandro Antonucci,Marco Zaffalon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduced FairMind, a tool for automated fairness analysis in datasets using causal methods and LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address fairness issues in AutoML frameworks that do not account for bias in training data and corresponding predictions.

Method: FairMind employs the standard fairness model based on counterfactual queries, preprocessing data for closed-form computations, and uses LLMs to generate fairness evaluation reports in a zero-shot setup.

Result: FairMind effectively evaluates dataset fairness, shows improvements over direct analysis by LLMs, and supports extensions to ordinal variables and continuous targets.

Conclusion: FairMind enhances fairness analysis in AutoML workflows, offering sound causal methods and practical extensions for broader applications.

Abstract: AutoML, intended as the process of automating the application of machine learning to real-world problems, is a key step for AI popularisation. Most AutoML frameworks are not accounting for the potential lack of fairness in the training data and in the corresponding predictions. We introduce \textsc{FairMind}, a software prototype aiming to automatise fairness analysis at the dataset level. We achieve that by resorting to the assumptions of the \emph{standard fairness model}, recently proposed by Plečko and Bareinboim. This allows for a sound fairness evaluation in terms of causal effects, based on \emph{counterfactual} queries involving the target, possibly confounders and mediators, and the different values of an input feature we regard as \emph{protected}. After the necessary data preprocessing, the tool implements a closed-form computation of the effects. LLMs are consequently exploited to generate accurate reports on the fairness levels detected in the training dataset. We achieve that in a zero-shot setup and show by examples the expected advantages with respect to a direct analysis performed by the LLM. To favour applications, extensions to ordinal protected variable and continuous targets and novel decomposition results are also discussed.

</details>


### [271] [ANCORA: Learning to Question via Manifold-Anchored Self-Play for Verifiable Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27644)
*Chengcao Yang,Jun Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces ANCORA, a framework that enables a language model to generate, solve, and self-improve on verifiable problems without human supervision, significantly outperforming prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to leverage language models in a self-improving paradigm where they can create testable problems, solve them, and learn autonomously, aiming for efficiency and robustness in problem-solving capabilities.

Method: ANCORA employs a Proposer-Solver architecture, self-distilled supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and a UCB-guided Curriculum DAG for iterative stabilization and learning, ensuring novel and verified outputs.

Result: ANCORA achieves a significant improvement in performance, with a pass@1 of 81.5% in the test-time-training setting, outperforming baseline methods like the PSV self-play approach by 15.8 points.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that language models can enhance their performance autonomously by creating and solving problems under ANCORA, pushing AI capabilities in problem generation and solving frameworks.

Abstract: We propose a paradigm shift from learning to answer to learning to question: can a language model generate verifiable problems, solve them, and turn the resulting feedback into self-improvement without human supervision? We introduce ANCORA, an anchored-curriculum framework in which a unified policy alternates between a Proposer that synthesizes novel specifications and a Solver that produces verified solutions. ANCORA rests on three load-bearing mechanisms: a two-level group-relative update that couples Proposer advantages across specifications with Solver advantages across solution attempts; iterative self-distilled SFT that projects the base model onto its valid-output manifold before RL; and a UCB-guided Curriculum DAG that grows only through strictly filtered, novel, Solver-verified specifications. These stabilizers are necessary because sparse verifier feedback otherwise drives Proposer collapse even under MLRL-aligned rewards. Instantiated in Verus, ANCORA lifts Dafny2Verus pass@1 from a 26.6% SFT baseline to 81.5% in the test-time-training setting under 0-shot evaluation, outperforming the PSV self-play baseline by 15.8 points despite PSV using 1-shot inference; in a separate transfer setting, training from Dafny2Verus seeds yields 36.2% and 17.2% pass@1 on held-out MBPP and HumanEval.

</details>


### [272] [Fidelity, Diversity, and Privacy: A Multi-Dimensional LLM Evaluation for Clinical Data Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27014)
*Guillermo Iglesias,Gema Bello-Orgaz,María Navas-Loro,Cristian Ramirez-Atencia,Mercè Salvador Robert,Enrique Baca-Garcia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes using synthetic data generation with Large Language Models (LLMs) to address the scarcity of annotated medical data in mental health, while ensuring privacy safety and clinical relevance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality annotated medical data in mental health is challenging to obtain due to privacy regulations restricting data sharing.

Method: Introduces a pipeline utilizing DeepSeek-R1, OpenBioLLM-Llama3, and Qwen 3.5 to generate synthetic mental health evaluation reports based on ICD-10 codes, with comprehensive evaluation criteria for quality and privacy.

Result: Generated synthetic reports were found to be clinically coherent, lexically diverse, and privacy-safe, providing effective augmentation for clinical NLP tasks.

Conclusion: Synthetic data generation using LLMs can expand data for mental health applications without breaching patient confidentiality, offering a viable alternative to traditional data collection approaches.

Abstract: The scarcity of high-quality annotated medical data, particularly in mental health, poses a significant bottleneck for training robust machine learning models. Privacy regulations restrict data sharing, making synthetic data generation a promising alternative. The use of Large Language Models (LLMs) in a data augmentation pipeline could be leveraged as an alternative in this field. In the proposed methodology, DeepSeek-R1, OpenBioLLM-Llama3 and Qwen 3.5 are used to generate synthetic mental health evaluation reports conditioned on specific International Classification of Diseases, Tenth Revision (ICD-10) codes. Because naive text generation can lead to mode collapse or privacy breaches (memorization), a comprehensive evaluation framework is introduced. The generated diagnostic texts are assessed across three dimensions: semantic fidelity, lexical diversity, and privacy/plagiarism. The results demonstrate that all models can generate clinically coherent, diverse, and privacy-safe synthetic reports, significantly expanding the available training data for clinical natural language processing tasks without compromising patient confidentiality.

</details>


### [273] [NORACL: Neurogenesis for Oracle-free Resource-Adaptive Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27031)
*Karthik Charan Raghunathan,Christian Metzner,Laura Kriener,Melika Payvand*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces NORACL, a method inspired by biological neurogenesis, to address the stability-plasticity dilemma in continual learning by enabling dynamic architectural growth based on task needs, outperforming fixed-capacity methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the stability-plasticity dilemma in continual learning, where models need to balance learning new tasks without disturbing previously learned ones. Existing methods rely on fixed architectures sized for unknown future requirements, leading to inefficiencies.

Method: NORACL monitors representational and plasticity saturation signals to trigger neuronal growth in a compact network, adapting dynamically to the demands of incoming tasks.

Result: NORACL achieves higher or comparable average accuracies compared to fixed oracle-sized baselines while utilizing fewer parameters, and demonstrates interpretable architecture growth based on task properties.

Conclusion: Adaptive neurogenesis in NORACL improves continual learning outcomes, pushing the stability-plasticity trade-off frontier, and explains why fixed-capacity networks lose efficiency as tasks accumulate.

Abstract: In a continual learning setting, we require a model to be plastic enough to learn a new task and stable enough to not disturb previously learned capabilities. We argue that this dilemma has an architectural root. A finite network has limited representational and plastic resources, yet the required capacity depends on properties of the future task stream that are unknown: how many tasks will be encountered, and how much they overlap in feature space. Regularization-based methods preserve past knowledge within fixed-capacity architectures and therefore implicitly rely on an oracle architecture sized for this unknown future. When tasks are only weakly related, fixed architectures progressively run out of plastic resources; when tasks are few or strongly overlapping, models are often over-provisioned. Inspired by neurogenesis in biology, we propose NORACL to address the stability-plasticity dilemma by tackling the oracle architecture problem through neuronal growth. Starting from a compact network, NORACL grows only when needed by monitoring two complementary signals for representational and plasticity saturation. We evaluate NORACL against oracle-sized static baselines across varying task counts and geometries. Across all settings, NORACL achieves final average accuracies that are better than or on par with oracle-provisioned static baselines while using fewer parameters. Additionally, NORACL yields architectures with interpretable growth, i.e. dissimilar tasks predominantly expand feature-extraction layers, whereas tasks which rely on common features shift growth toward later feature-combination layers. Our analysis further explains why fixed-capacity networks lose plasticity as tasks accumulate, whereas NORACL creates fresh capacity for new tasks through growth. Together, these results show that adaptive neurogenesis pushes the stability-plasticity Pareto frontier of continual learning.

</details>


### [274] [Dynamic Adversarial Fine-Tuning Reorganizes Refusal Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27019)
*Wenhao Lan,Shan Li,Junbin Yang,Haihua Shen,Yijun Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores how safety-aligned language models manage the balance between refusal of harmful requests and avoiding over-refusal, using adversarial fine-tuning methods and measurements of data. It focuses on understanding training-time mechanisms rather than proposing a solution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the unclear training-time mechanisms behind the tradeoff between refusing harmful queries and over-refusal in language models, and to gain insights into adversarial fine-tuning's impact on refusal dynamics.

Method: The authors conducted a measurement-driven study using a single 7B model backbone trained under both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and R2D2-style adversarial fine-tuning. They used benchmarks like HarmBench, StrongREJECT, and XSTest to analyze refusal performance, combined with geometric and causal methods.

Result: R2D2 fine-tuning achieves strong initial refusal accuracy but later reopens to partial acceptance, suggesting dynamic changes. Geometrically, refusal carriers shift layers during training, and causal interventions reveal low-dimensional but utility-coupled control.

Conclusion: The paper supports a reorganization mechanism behind changes in refusal capability rather than simple drift. Insights are limited to one model architecture and fixed-source attacks, suggesting further research is necessary.

Abstract: Safety-aligned language models must refuse harmful requests without collapsing into broad over-refusal, but the training-time mechanisms behind this tradeoff remain unclear. Prior work characterizes refusal directions and jailbreak robustness, yet does not explain how dynamic adversarial fine-tuning changes refusal carriers across training. We present a measurement-driven mechanism study, not a new defense, on one 7B backbone under supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and R2D2-style dynamic adversarial fine-tuning. Our protocol aligns fixed-source HarmBench, StrongREJECT, and XSTest with a five-anchor refusal-geometry suite and causal interventions. R2D2 drives fixed-source HarmBench ASR to 0.000 at steps 50 and 100, then partially reopens to 0.035 at step 250 and 0.250 at step 500; SFT remains less robust, with ASR between 0.505 and 0.588 at the same anchors. On XSTest, R2D2 any-refusal is 1.000 early, then falls to 0.664 and 0.228. Geometrically, R2D2 preserves a late-layer admissible carrier through step 100 before relocating to an early-layer carrier, while effective rank remains near 1.23--1.27. Causal interventions indicate low-dimensional but utility-coupled control. These results support a reorganization account rather than a drift-only account, with evidence limited to one backbone and fixed-source attacks.

</details>


### [275] [Learning to Forget: Continual Learning with Adaptive Weight Decay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27063)
*Aditya A. Ramesh,Alex Lewandowski,Jürgen Schmidhuber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Forgetting through Adaptive Decay (FADE), an approach to dynamically adjust weight decay rates for continual learning systems, ensuring better retention and learning balance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods of weight decay treat all parameters and time periods uniformly, resulting in suboptimal performance for systems handling both stable knowledge and rapidly changing targets.

Method: FADE dynamically determines per-parameter decay rates through approximate meta-gradient descent, applied to the final layer of neural networks.

Result: FADE identifies varied decay rates for different parameters, complements step-size adaptation, and outperforms fixed weight decay methods in online tracking and streaming classification tasks.

Conclusion: FADE enhances continual learning by effectively controlling forgetting and improving adaptability in dynamic environments.

Abstract: Continual learning agents with finite capacity must balance acquiring new knowledge with retaining the old. This requires controlled forgetting of knowledge that is no longer needed, freeing up capacity to learn. Weight decay, viewed as a mechanism for forgetting, can serve this role by gradually discarding information stored in the weights. However, a fixed scalar weight decay drives this forgetting uniformly over time and uniformly across all parameters, even when some encode stable knowledge while others track rapidly changing targets. We introduce Forgetting through Adaptive Decay (FADE), which adapts per-parameter weight decay rates online via approximate meta-gradient descent. We derive FADE for the online linear setting and apply it to the final layer of neural networks. Our empirical analysis shows that FADE automatically discovers distinct decay rates for different parameters, complements step-size adaptation, and consistently improves over fixed weight decay across online tracking and streaming classification problems.

</details>


### [276] [Learning Rate Transfer in Normalized Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27077)
*Boris Shigida,Boris Hanin,Andrey Gromov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces πιοGPT, a novel parameterization of the normalized transformer (nGPT), enabling learning rate transfer across width, depth, and token horizon.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of learning rate transfer in nGPT across model dimension and token horizon.

Method: By combining numerical experiments and alignment exponents to refine the μP hyperparameter transfer approach.

Result: νGPT demonstrates enhanced empirical performance with learning rate transfer capabilities.

Conclusion: Updating the nGPT parameterization to νGPT resolves its limitations, offering scalability in learning rate transfer across different dimensions.

Abstract: The Normalized Transformer, or nGPT (arXiv:2410.01131) achieves impressive training speedups and does not require weight decay or learning rate warmup. However, despite having hyperparameters that explicitly scale with model size, we observe that nGPT does not exhibit learning rate transfer across model dimension and token horizon. To rectify this, we combine numerical experiments with a principled use of alignment exponents (arXiv:2407.05872) to revisit and modify the $μ$P approach to hyperparameter transfer (arXiv:2011.14522). The result is a novel nGPT parameterization we call $ν$GPT. Through extensive empirical validation, we find $ν$GPT exhibits learning rate transfer across width, depth, and token horizon.

</details>


### [277] [When Does Structure Matter in Continual Learning? Dimensionality Controls When Modularity Shapes Representational Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27656)
*Kathrin Korte,Joachim Winter Pedersen,Eleni Nisioti,Sebastian Risi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates how network architecture, task similarity, and representational dimensionality impact continual learning systems, emphasizing the importance of modular network structures in low-dimensional learning regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the stability-plasticity dilemma in continual learning and understand when structural separation aids task representation management.

Method: Authors compared modular and single-module recurrent networks while varying task similarity and weight initialization to explore representational dimensionality effects.

Result: In high-dimensional regimes, architecture had minimal influence, but in low-dimensional regimes, modular networks showed graded alignment and better task separation compared to single-module networks.

Conclusion: Representational dimensionality governs the relevance of architectural separation, and adaptive geometry is vital for designing effective continual learning systems.

Abstract: To preserve previously learned representations, continual learning systems must strike a balance between plasticity, the ability to acquire new knowledge, and stability. This stability-plasticity dilemma affects how representations can be reused across tasks: shared structure enables transfer when tasks are similar but may also induce interference when new learning disrupts existing representations. However, it remains unclear when and why structural separation influences this trade-off. In this study, we examine how network architecture, task similarity, and representational dimensionality jointly shape learning in a sequential task paradigm inspired by transfer-interference studies. We compare a task-partitioned modular recurrent network with a single-module baseline by systematically varying task similarity (low, medium, high) and the scale of weight initialization, which induces different learning regimes that we empirically characterize through the effective dimensionality of the learned representations. We find that architecture has minimal impact in high-dimensional regimes where representations are sufficiently unconstrained to accommodate multiple tasks without strong interference. In contrast, in lower-dimensional (rich) regimes, architectural separation is decisive: modular networks exhibit graded alignment of task-specific subspaces with overlap for similar tasks, partial orthogonalization for moderately dissimilar tasks, and stronger separation for dissimilar tasks. This graded geometry is absent in the single network baseline. Our findings suggest that representational dimensionality acts as a key organizing variable governing when structural separation becomes functionally relevant, and highlight adaptive geometry as a central principle for designing continual learning systems.

</details>


### [278] [Cross-Subject Generalization for EEG Decoding: A Survey of Deep Learning Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27033)
*Taida Li,Yujun Yan,Fei Dou,Wenzhan Song,Xiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A survey on deep learning methods for addressing cross-subject variability in EEG decoding, categorized into key methodological approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome challenges posed by high inter-subject variability and domain shifts in cross-subject EEG decoding.

Method: The study categorizes methodologies like feature alignment, adversarial learning, feature disentanglement, and contrastive learning, supported by a subject-independent evaluation framework.

Result: Provides a taxonomy of methodologies addressing the cross-subject EEG decoding challenge and analyzes critical elements for robust real-world applications.

Conclusion: Advances in cross-subject EEG decoding require confronting theoretical limitations, leveraging subject identity, and exploring EEG foundation models.

Abstract: Deep learning for cross-subject EEG decoding is hindered by high inter-subject variability, which introduces a severe domain shift between training and unseen test subjects. This survey presents a comprehensive review of deep learning methodologies specifically engineered to address this cross-subject generalization challenge. To ground this analysis, we formalize the cross-subject setting as a multi-source domain problem and delineate the rigorous, subject-independent evaluation protocols required for valid assessment. Central to this survey is a systematic taxonomy of the current literature into discrete methodological families, including feature alignment, adversarial learning, feature disentanglement, and contrastive learning. We conclude by examining three critical elements for advancing robust, real-world decoding: the theoretical limitations of current methodologies, the structural value of subject identity, and the emergence of EEG foundation models.

</details>


### [279] [Physical Foundation Models: Fixed hardware implementations of large-scale neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27911)
*Logan G Wright,Tianyu Wang,Tatsuhiro Onodera,Peter L. McMahon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes leveraging "Physical Foundation Models" (PFMs), hardware implementations directly designed for large-scale neural networks, to achieve massive gains in energy efficiency, speed, and parameter density.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To capitalize on the universal applicability of foundation models and reduce the computational and energy demands of high-parameter models used in AI applications.

Method: Advocates implementing neural network designs directly in physical hardware, such as optical devices or nanostructured materials, to improve their operational efficiency and scalability.

Result: Indicates the feasibility of creating physical designs for trillion-parameter models and potentially scaling to sizes exceeding today's limits ($10^{15}$-$10^{18}$ parameters).

Conclusion: PFMs present an opportunity to revolutionize AI hardware, enabling ultra-large models to operate efficiently and feasibly, but require overcoming significant research and design challenges.

Abstract: Foundation models are deep neural networks (such as GPT-5, Gemini~3, and Opus~4) trained on large datasets that can perform diverse downstream tasks -- text and code generation, question answering, summarization, image classification, and so on. The philosophy of foundation models is to put effort into a single, large (${\sim}10^{12}$-parameter) general-purpose model that can be adapted to many downstream tasks with no or minimal additional training. We argue that the rise of foundation models presents an opportunity for hardware engineers: in contrast to when different models were used for different tasks, it now makes sense to build special-purpose, fixed hardware implementations of neural networks, manufactured and released at the roughly 1-year cadence of major new foundation-model versions.
  Beyond conventional digital-electronic inference hardware with read-only weight memory, we advocate a more radical re-thinking: hardware in which the neural network is realized directly at the level of the physical design and operates via the hardware's natural physical dynamics -- \textit{Physical Foundation Models} (PFMs). PFMs could enable orders-of-magnitude advantages in energy efficiency, speed, and parameter density. For ${\sim}10^{12}$-parameter models, this would both reduce the high energy burden of AI in datacenters and enable AI in edge devices that today are power-constrained to far smaller models. PFMs could also enable inference hardware for models much larger than current ones: $10^{15}$- or even $10^{18}$-parameter PFMs seem plausible by some measures. We present back-of-the-envelope calculations illustrating PFM scaling using an optical example -- a 3D nanostructured glass medium -- and discuss prospects in nanoelectronics and other physical platforms. We conclude with the major research challenges that must be resolved for trillion-parameter PFMs and beyond to become reality.

</details>


### [280] [Detecting Clinical Discrepancies in Health Coaching Agents: A Dual-Stream Memory and Reconciliation Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27045)
*Samuel L Pugh,Eric Yang,Alexander Muir Sutherland,Alessandra Breschi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses the challenge of reconciling patient self-reports and Electronic Health Records (EHRs) in longitudinal healthcare systems using a Dual-Stream Memory Architecture with a specialized Reconciliation Engine.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM systems face risks in managing longitudinal healthcare journeys because they prioritize coherence by overwriting older facts with newer patient reports, which can compromise safety when dealing with clinical data.

Method: A Dual-Stream Memory Architecture is proposed, separating patient narratives and structured clinical records, with a Reconciliation Engine evaluating discrepancies based on type, severity, and FHIR resources.

Result: The system was tested on 26 patients across 675 wellness coaching sessions using a hybrid dataset, achieving 84.4% detection of discrepancies and 86.7% safety-critical recall. The study identified a 13.6% error cascade attributed mainly to memory extraction from unstructured conversation.

Conclusion: Validating patient-reported data against clinical records is both necessary and feasible for the safe implementation of longitudinal health agents.

Abstract: As Large Language Model (LLM) agents transition from single-session tools to persistent systems managing longitudinal healthcare journeys, their memory architectures face a critical challenge: reconciling two imperfect sources of truth. The patient's evolving self-report is current but prone to recall bias, while the Electronic Health Record (EHR) is medically validated but frequently stale. General-purpose agent memory systems optimize for coherence by overwriting older facts with the user's latest statement, a pattern that risks safety failures when applied to clinical data. We introduce a Dual-Stream Memory Architecture that strictly separates the patient narrative from the structured clinical record (FHIR), governed by a dedicated Reconciliation Engine that evaluates every extracted memory against the patient's FHIR profile and classifies discrepancies by type, severity, and the specific FHIR resources involved. We evaluate this architecture on 26 patients across 675 longitudinal wellness coaching sessions, using a hybrid dataset that interleaves real provider-patient transcripts with synthetic, FHIR-grounded clinical scenarios. In isolated testing, the engine detects 84.4% of designed clinical discrepancies with 86.7% safety-critical recall. By coupling extraction and reconciliation evaluation on the same data, we directly quantify a 13.6% error cascade, tracing the degradation to clinical details lost during memory extraction from unstructured conversation rather than to downstream classification errors. These findings establish that validating patient-reported memories against clinical records is both feasible and necessary for safe deployment of longitudinal health agents.

</details>


### [281] [Optimized Deferral for Imbalanced Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27723)
*Corinna Cortes,Anqi Mao,Mehryar Mohri,Yutao Zhong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces MILD, a novel algorithm to address expert imbalance in two-stage learning to defer tasks, improving performance across various domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations in learning to defer systems, particularly the expert imbalance problem, which limits their effectiveness in domains requiring balanced resource allocation, such as natural language generation and medical diagnosis.

Method: The authors framed deferral loss optimization as a cost-sensitive learning problem and proposed new margin-based loss functions and guarantees. They developed specialized algorithms named MILD tailored for handling expert imbalance in two-stage learning to defer systems.

Result: MILD demonstrates significant performance gains over baseline methods in both image classification and LLM routing tasks under expert imbalance scenarios.

Conclusion: MILD effectively handles scenarios of expert imbalance in learning to defer tasks, optimizing accuracy and efficiency by improving the selection of experts in computational systems.

Abstract: Learning algorithms can be significantly improved by routing complex or uncertain inputs to specialized experts, balancing accuracy with computational cost. This approach, known as learning to defer, is essential in domains like natural language generation, medical diagnosis, and computer vision, where an effective deferral can reduce errors at low extra resource consumption. However, the two-stage learning to defer setting, which leverages existing predictors such as a collection of LLMs or other classifiers, often faces challenges due to an expert imbalance problem. This imbalance can lead to suboptimal performance, with deferral algorithms favoring the majority expert. We present a comprehensive study of two-stage learning to defer in expert imbalance settings. We cast the deferral loss optimization as a novel cost-sensitive learning problem over the input-expert domain. We derive new margin-based loss functions and guarantees tailored to this setting, and develop novel algorithms for cost-sensitive learning. Leveraging these results, we design principled deferral algorithms, MILD (Margin-based Imbalanced Learning to Defer), specifically suited for expert imbalance settings. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, showing clear improvements over existing baselines on both image classification and real-world Large Language Model (LLM) routing tasks.

</details>


### [282] [Mind the Gap: Structure-Aware Consistency in Preference Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27733)
*Mehryar Mohri,Yutao Zhong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper highlights inconsistencies in standard surrogate losses for preference learning in aligning LLMs and proposes a margin-shifted ranking framework (SA-DPO) to address this issue, offering improved guarantees for capacity-limited models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the theoretical inconsistency and limited generalization guarantees of existing surrogate methods used in aligning LLMs with human intent via preference learning.

Method: Developed a margin-shifted ranking framework with $H$-consistency bounds, proposed a new SA-DPO objective to handle semantic distances, and introduced Margin-Capacity Profile to study trade-offs between consistency and model limitations.

Result: Showed that standard surrogates in preference learning are inconsistent and established that heavy-tailed surrogates provide better consistency guarantees for models with limited capacity.

Conclusion: The proposed framework and objectives address the limitations of existing methods, improving consistency and alignment in LLMs, especially for capacity-constrained models.

Abstract: Preference learning has become the foundation of aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with human intent. Popular methods, such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), minimize surrogate losses as proxies for the intractable pairwise ranking loss. However, we demonstrate that for the equicontinuous hypothesis sets typical of neural networks, these standard surrogates are theoretically inconsistent, yielding vacuous generalization guarantees. To resolve this, we formulate LLM alignment within a margin-shifted ranking framework. We derive rigorous $H$-consistency bounds that depend on enforcing a separation margin $γ$. Crucially, we extend this to Structure-Aware $H$-consistency, introducing a novel objective (SA-DPO) that adapts the margin based on the semantic distance between responses to handle synonyms and hard pairs. Finally, we analyze the trade-off between consistency and model limitations via the Margin-Capacity Profile, proving that heavy-tailed surrogates (such as the Polynomial Hinge family) offer superior consistency guarantees for capacity-bounded models compared to the standard logistic loss used in DPO.

</details>


### [283] [Co-Evolving Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27083)
*Naibin Gu,Chenxu Yang,Qingyi Si,Chuanyu Qin,Dingyu Yao,Peng Fu,Zheng Lin,Weiping Wang,Nan Duan,Jiaqi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Co-Evolving Policy Distillation (CoPD), a unified approach to integrate multiple expert capabilities into a single model, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address capability loss issues in current methods like mixed RLVR and OPD when consolidating multiple expert capabilities into one model.

Method: CoPD promotes parallel training of experts, incorporating bidirectional OPD during ongoing training, enabling mutual teaching and co-evolution to mitigate behavioral pattern gaps and divergence costs.

Result: Experiments show that CoPD achieves superior performance in integrating text, image, and video reasoning capabilities compared to existing methods and even domain-specific experts.

Conclusion: CoPD provides an effective integration mechanism for expert capabilities, potentially inspiring new paradigms for training scaling in AI.

Abstract: RLVR and OPD have become standard paradigms for post-training. We provide a unified analysis of these two paradigms in consolidating multiple expert capabilities into a single model, identifying capability loss in different ways: mixed RLVR suffers from inter-capability divergence cost, while the pipeline of first training experts and then performing OPD, though avoiding divergence, fails to fully absorb teacher capabilities due to large behavioral pattern gaps between teacher and student. We propose Co-Evolving Policy Distillation (CoPD), which encourages parallel training of experts and introduces OPD during each expert's ongoing RLVR training rather than after complete expert training, with experts serving as mutual teachers (making OPD bidirectional) to co-evolve. This enables more consistent behavioral patterns among experts while maintaining sufficient complementary knowledge throughout. Experiments validate that CoPD achieves all-in-one integration of text, image, and video reasoning capabilities, significantly outperforming strong baselines such as mixed RLVR and MOPD, and even surpassing domain-specific experts. The model parallel training pattern offered by CoPD may inspire a novel training scaling paradigm.

</details>


### [284] [Linear-Core Surrogates: Smooth Loss Functions with Linear Rates for Classification and Structured Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27742)
*Mehryar Mohri,Yutao Zhong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Linear-Core (LC) Surrogates, a family of convex loss functions that address the trade-off between optimization speed and statistical efficiency in classification tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the limitations of smooth losses (slow consistency bounds) and piecewise-linear losses (non-differentiability) by developing a loss function that combines the benefits of both.

Method: The authors propose LC Surrogates, which feature a linear core combined with a smooth tail, resulting in loss functions that are differentiable everywhere. These surrogates retain linear $H$-consistency bounds and enable efficient stochastic gradient estimation.

Result: LC Surrogates demonstrate superior computational efficiency and robustness. They provide a 23x speedup in structured prediction and outperform Cross-Entropy by 2.6% on noisy CIFAR-10 datasets.

Conclusion: This approach effectively balances optimization rates and statistical consistency, offering computational and robustness advantages in both classification and structured prediction tasks.

Abstract: The choice of loss function in classification involves a fundamental trade-off: smooth losses (like Cross-Entropy) enable fast optimization rates but yield slow square-root consistency bounds, while piecewise-linear losses (like Hinge) offer fast linear consistency rates but suffer from non-differentiability. We propose Linear-Core (LC) Surrogates, a new family of convex loss functions that resolve this tension by stitching a linear core to a smooth tail. We prove that these surrogates are differentiable everywhere while retaining strict linear $H$-consistency bounds, effectively combining the optimization benefits of smoothness with the statistical efficiency of margin-based losses. In the structured prediction setting, we show that this smoothness unlocks a massive computational and energy advantage: it allows for an unbiased stochastic gradient estimator that bypasses the quadratic complexity $O(|\mathscr{Y}|^2)$ of exact inference (e.g., Viterbi). Empirically, our method achieves a 23$\times$ speedup over Structured SVMs on large-vocabulary sequence tagging tasks and demonstrates superior robustness to instance-dependent label noise, outperforming Cross-Entropy by 2.6% on corrupted CIFAR-10.

</details>


### [285] [Anomaly Detection in Soil Heavy Metal Contamination Using Unsupervised Learning for Environmental Risk Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27102)
*Isaac Tettey Adjokatse,Samuel Senyo Koranteng,George Yamoah Afrifa,Theophilus Ansah-Narh,Marcellin Atemkeng,Joseph Bremang Tandoh,Kow Ahor Essel-Yorke,Richmond Opoku-Sarkodie,Rebecca Davis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The study utilized unsupervised machine learning to isolate anomalous heavy metal contamination patterns in soils from waste sites in Ghana, highlighting environmental risks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rapid urbanization in Ghana has increased unregulated waste disposal leading to severe soil contamination risks due to heavy metals.

Method: Unsupervised machine learning, including Isolation Forest, PCA reconstruction error, and DBSCAN, was used to detect contamination patterns associated with health risks in soil samples.

Result: Six robust anomalies were identified, concentrated at one site (S3). Anomalies showed higher Hazard Index values and significant health risk links due to heavy metal contamination.

Conclusion: Unsupervised machine learning effectively identifies contamination patterns, offering objective insights for environmental risk management and site prioritization.

Abstract: Soil contamination by heavy metals poses a persistent environmental and public health concern in rapidly urbanising regions of Ghana, particularly at unregulated waste disposal sites. This study applies an unsupervised machine learning framework to detect and characterise anomalous heavy metal contamination patterns in soils from twelve waste sites and residential controls in the Central Region, of Ghana. Concentrations of eight metals (As, Cd, Cr, Cu, Hg, Ni, Pb, Zn) were analysed alongside standard health risk indices, including the Hazard Index (HI) and Incremental Lifetime Cancer Risk (ILCR). Isolation Forest and PCA reconstruction error each identified $12$ anomalous samples ($15.4\%$ of $78$ samples), while DBSCAN detected no density-isolated noise points. A consensus approach isolated six robust anomalies ($7.7\%)$, all spatially concentrated at a single site (S3). Anomalies exhibited approximately $70$--$80\%$ higher mean HI values than normal samples, with all consensus anomalies exceeding the HI$=1$ threshold. PCA reconstruction error showed a strong positive association with HI ($r \approx 0.8$), indicating consistency between multivariate deviation and health risk. Three distinct anomaly types were identified: extreme Cu enrichment at S3, anomalously low Ni at S4/S5, and moderate multi-metal (Pb--Zn) co-elevation at S9--S12. The results demonstrate that unsupervised machine learning provides granular, objective insight beyond aggregate indices, enabling targeted site prioritisation and risk-informed environmental management.

</details>


### [286] [Kernelized Advantage Estimation: From Nonparametric Statistics to LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.28005)
*Shijin Gong,Kai Ye,Jin Zhu,Xinyu Zhang,Hongyi Zhou,Chengchun Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a kernel smoothing method for value estimation in large language model training, offering improved sample and computational efficiency for reinforcement learning under constrained settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the computational and memory challenges of current reinforcement learning methods in large language models, particularly under resource constraints where only a small number of reasoning traces can be sampled per prompt.

Method: The authors bring nonparametric statistical methods, specifically kernel smoothing, to estimate the value function and optimize policies for LLM reasoning, aiming at low-variance gradient estimation in practical scenarios.

Result: Numerical and theoretical results indicate that the proposed method achieves accurate value and gradient estimations while enabling more efficient policy optimization.

Conclusion: The proposed kernel smoothing approach introduces a statistically and computationally efficient solution to reinforcement learning in LLMs, striking a balance between sample efficiency and computational cost.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have increasingly relied on reinforcement learning (RL) to improve their reasoning capabilities. Three approaches have been widely adopted: (i) Proximal policy optimization and advantage actor-critic rely on a deep neural network to estimate the value function of the learning policy in order to reduce the variance of the policy gradient. However, estimating and maintaining such a value network incurs substantial computational and memory overhead. (ii) Group relative policy optimization (GRPO) avoids training a value network by approximating the value function using sample averages. However, GRPO samples a large number of reasoning traces per prompt to achieve accurate value function approximation, making it computationally expensive. (iii) REINFORCE-type algorithms sample only a single reasoning trajectory per prompt, which reduces computational cost but suffers from poor sample efficiency.
  In this work, we focus on a practical, resource-constrained setting in which only a small number of reasoning traces can be sampled per prompt, while low-variance gradient estimation remains essential for high-quality policy learning. To address this challenge, we bring classical nonparametric statistical methods, which are both computationally and statistically efficient, to LLM reasoning. We employ kernel smoothing as a concrete example for value function estimation and the subsequent policy optimization. Numerical and theoretical results demonstrate that our proposal achieves accurate value and gradient estimation, leading to improved policy optimization.

</details>


### [287] [Better Models, Faster Training: Sigmoid Attention for single-cell Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27124)
*Vijay Sadashivaiah,Georgios Dasoulas,Judith Mueller,Soumya Ghosh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes using sigmoid attention instead of softmax attention for training stable biological foundation models, leading to better performance, faster training, and improved stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the instabilities and inefficiencies in training biological foundation models using softmax attention, which often leads to issues like exploding gradients and dense coupling.

Method: The method involves replacing softmax attention with sigmoid attention, leveraging its globally bounded derivatives and diagonal Jacobian structure. They also introduce TritonSigmoid, a specially designed GPU kernel for efficient computation.

Result: The results show that sigmoid attention achieves 25% better performance in learned representations, with faster (up to 10%) and more stable training. It also outperforms softmax in stress tests and achieves high computational efficiency with the new TritonSigmoid kernel.

Conclusion: Sigmoid attention is theoretically more stable and empirically superior for biological foundation models, making it a viable replacement for softmax attention.

Abstract: Training stable biological foundation models requires rethinking attention mechanisms: we find that using sigmoid attention as a drop in replacement for softmax attention a) produces better learned representations: on six diverse single-cell datasets, sigmoid achieves 25% higher cell-type separation, better cell-type cohesion metrics, and lower validation loss, b) faster training, models with sigmoid attention train up to 10% faster than their softmax counterparts, and c) more stable training by eliminating inherent sources of instability in softmax attention. We establish that sigmoid attention has globally bounded derivatives ($\leq 0.25$) as opposed to softmax, and a diagonal Jacobian structure in contrast with softmax's dense coupling, which together help alleviate training instabilities. In stress tests on 160M-parameter bidirectional attention models trained without gradient clipping on 8K-token sequences, softmax diverges catastrophically, with gradients exploding by four orders of magnitude, while sigmoid remains stable. Finally, we implement and open-source TritonSigmoid, an efficient GPU kernel that achieves 515 TFLOPS on H100 GPUs, outperforming both FlashAttention-2 and FlashSigmoid, with native padding support, which is essential for biological sequences. Our results establish sigmoid attention as both theoretically grounded and empirically superior for biological foundation models. Code is available at https://github.com/MSDLLCpapers/triton-sigmoid

</details>


### [288] [How to Guide Your Flow: Few-Step Alignment via Flow Map Reward Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27147)
*Jerry Y. Huang,Justin Lin,Sheel Shah,Kartik Nair,Nicholas M. Boffi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces FMRG, a unique single-trajectory framework for guidance in generative modeling that achieves faster and improved results in tasks like text-to-image generation and style transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative modeling often requires producing samples aligned with specific rewards such as aesthetic quality or human preferences, but current methods are either computationally expensive or based on weak approximations.

Method: The authors reformulate the guidance process as a deterministic optimal control problem and introduce FMRG, a method that leverages the flow map for efficient integration and guidance without requiring additional training.

Result: FMRG achieves comparable or superior results to existing baseline methods across tasks (such as style transfer and inverse problems) with dramatically reduced computational requirements, achieving a speedup of at least an order of magnitude.

Conclusion: FMRG provides a fast and effective guidance approach in generative modeling, bridging gaps in efficiency and performance while using single-trajectory frameworks.

Abstract: In generative modeling, we often wish to produce samples that maximize a user-specified reward such as aesthetic quality or alignment with human preferences, a problem known as guidance. Despite their widespread use, existing guidance methods either require expensive multi-particle, many-step schemes or rely on poorly understood approximations. We reformulate guidance as a deterministic optimal control problem, yielding a hierarchy of algorithms that subsumes existing approaches at the coarsest level. We show that the flow map, an object of significant recent interest for its role in fast inference, arises naturally in the optimal solution. Based on this observation, we propose Flow Map Reward Guidance (FMRG): a training-free, single-trajectory framework that uses the flow map to both integrate and guide the flow. At text-to-image scale, FMRG matches or surpasses baselines across inverse problems, style transfer, human preferences, and VLM rewards with as few as 3 NFEs, giving at least an order-of-magnitude speedup in comparison to prior state of the art.

</details>


### [289] [ConformaDecompose: Explaining Uncertainty via Calibration Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27149)
*Fatima Rabia Yapicioglu,Meltem Aksoy,Alberto Rigenti,Tuwe Löfström-Cavallin,Helena Löfström-Cavallin,Seyda Yoncaci,Luca Longo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework to explain the uncertainty in Conformal Prediction by breaking down its components and localizing calibration support around individual test instances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conformal Prediction provides reliable prediction intervals but lacks insight into the sources of uncertainty in these intervals, particularly distinguishing between irreducible and reducible uncertainties.

Method: An uncertainty-aware explainability framework is developed, which uses progressive calibration localization to analyze and disentangle epistemic uncertainty in conformal prediction for regression tasks.

Result: The framework reveals how conformal intervals contract and stabilize around test instances, aligns with epistemic uncertainty proxies, and provides insights into the varying contributions of uncertainties across different tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed method enhances the interpretability of conformal intervals by explaining uncertainty sources at an instance level, without modifying the predictor or jeopardizing coverage guarantees.

Abstract: Conformal Prediction provides distribution-free prediction intervals with guaranteed coverage, but its reliance on a single global calibration threshold obscures the sources of uncertainty at the instance level. In particular, it conflates irreducible noise with uncertainty induced by heterogeneous training data (aleatoric), model limitations, or calibration mismatch (epistemic), offering little insight into why an interval is wide or whether it could be reduced. We introduce an uncertainty-aware explainability framework that analyses the reducibility of calibration-induced epistemic conformal uncertainty via progressive calibration localisation for regression tasks. The approach is diagnostic rather than causal: it does not estimate true aleatoric or epistemic uncertainty, but explains how conformal intervals contract and stabilise as calibration support is localised around a test instance. Across benchmarks and real-world data, absolute reducible uncertainty aligns with epistemic proxies, while its relative contribution varies by task, revealing regimes hidden by interval width. This instance-level view complements conformal uncertainty, enhancing interpretability without altering the predictor or coverage.

</details>


### [290] [Generalizing the Geometry of Model Merging Through Frechet Averages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27155)
*Marvin F. da Silva,Mohammed Adnan,Felix Dangel,Sageev Oore*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Fréchet averaging for model merging by addressing architectural symmetries, suggesting an innovative approach using quotient manifold geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model merging without extra training often fails due to architectural symmetries; this paper aims to address those challenges by reevaluating merging geometries and procedures.

Method: The authors introduce Fréchet averaging, leveraging geodesic distances on manifolds to merge models symmetrically. They focus specifically on low-rank adapters (LoRA) and outline limitations and practical algorithms for merging.

Result: Fréchet averaging, aligned with symmetry considerations, extends Fisher merging and outperforms some existing methods, especially in LoRA settings.

Conclusion: Fréchet averaging is a promising symmetry-aware model merging method that redefines effective distance metrics, showcasing superior results under specific structural settings.

Abstract: Model merging aims to combine multiple models into one without additional training. Naïve parameter-space averaging can be fragile under architectural symmetries, as their geometry does not take them into account. In this work we show that not only the geometry, but also the averaging procedure itself, must be symmetry-invariant to achieve symmetry-aware merges. Consequently, we propose a general solution: merging as Fréchet averaging, i.e., selecting parameters that minimize a sum of geodesic distances on an appropriate manifold. In this view, the key design choice is the overall geometry, i.e., the choice of metric, manifold, and distance approximation, that determines what it means for two models to be "close". We show that Fréchet averaging, combined with simplifying assumptions, contains Fisher merging. Building on this, we examine the particular case of low-rank adapters (LoRA), whose symmetries induce a distinct geometry: that of a quotient manifold. We outline the limitations of current LoRA merging methods, propose a practical algorithm for this setting, and show how they compare with other commonly used approaches.

</details>


### [291] [Distributional Alignment Games for Answer-Level Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27166)
*Mehryar Mohri,Jon Schneider,Yifan Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses optimizing language models based on the quality of their final answers rather than their reasoning processes, using a game-theoretical framework to make the problem computationally efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods struggle to optimize language models for answer-level correctness due to computational challenges in assessing the vast space of reasoning paths.

Method: The authors develop a game-theoretical approach—a Distributional Alignment Game—where optimization is framed as a two-player game between a generator and an auxiliary target distribution, focusing on tractable solutions.

Result: They show that their method simplifies the marginalization problem and incorporates it into efficient algorithms (e.g., Coherence-GRPO), achieving gains in mathematical reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a unified, computationally efficient way to optimize language models for answer-level correctness, advancing the field of language model fine-tuning.

Abstract: We focus on the problem of \emph{Answer-Level Fine-Tuning} (ALFT), where the goal is to optimize a language model based on the correctness or properties of its final answers, rather than the specific reasoning traces used to produce them. Directly optimizing answer-level objectives is computationally intractable due to the need to marginalize over the vast space of latent reasoning paths. To overcome this, we propose a general game-theoretical framework that lifts the problem to a \emph{Distributional Alignment Game}. We formulate ALFT as a two-player game between a Policy (the generator) and a Target (an auxiliary distribution). We prove that the Nash Equilibrium of this game corresponds exactly to the solution of the original answer-level optimization problem. This variational perspective transforms the intractable marginalization problem into a tractable projection problem. We demonstrate that this framework unifies recent approaches to diversity and self-improvement (coherence) and provide efficient algorithms compatible with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), such as Coherence-GRPO, yielding significant complexity gains in mathematical reasoning tasks.

</details>


### [292] [Context-Aware Graph Attention for Unsupervised Telco Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27172)
*Sara Malacarne,Eirik Hoel-Høiseth,Erlend Aune,David Zsolt Biro,Massimiliano Ruocco*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The study introduces C-MTAD-GAT, an unsupervised graph-attention-based model aimed at anomaly detection in multivariate time series data from mobile networks. It improves upon state-of-the-art techniques and proves effectiveness in real-world industrial applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Anomaly detection in multivariate time series data from mobile networks is challenging, especially with the need for unsupervised methods that perform well without labeled data.

Method: The researchers propose C-MTAD-GAT, which uses graph attention mechanisms combined with lightweight context embeddings. It pairs a deterministic reconstruction head with multi-step forecasting to generate anomaly scores. Detection thresholds are calibrated using validation residuals without requiring labels.

Result: C-MTAD-GAT outperforms state-of-the-art models MTAD-GAT and DC-VAE on the TELCO dataset in event-level and pointwise F1 scores while producing fewer false alarms. It is successfully deployed in a national mobile operator's core network.

Conclusion: The model demonstrates robust, industry-ready performance for anomaly detection in mobile networks, making it a strong candidate for real-world deployment.

Abstract: We propose C-MTAD-GAT, an \emph{unsupervised}, \emph{context-aware} graph-attention model for anomaly detection in multivariate time series from mobile networks. C-MTAD-GAT combines graph attention with lightweight context embeddings, and uses a deterministic reconstruction head and multi-step forecaster to produce anomaly scores. Detection thresholds are calibrated \emph{without labels} from validation residuals, keeping the pipeline fully unsupervised. On the public TELCO dataset, C-MTAD-GAT consistently outperforms MTAD-GAT and the Telco-specific DC-VAE, two state-of-the-art baselines, in both event-level and pointwise F1, while triggering substantially fewer alarms. C-MTAD-GAT is also deployed in the Core network of a national mobile operator, demonstrating its resilience in real industrial settings.

</details>


### [293] [Preserving Temporal Dynamics in Time Series Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27182)
*Ci Lin,Futong Li,Tet Yeap,Iluju Kiringa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses the mismatch in temporal dynamics when using GANs for synthetic time-series data generation and proposes a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)-based framework to preserve temporal dynamics and mitigate distribution shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GAN-based methods for synthetic time-series generation often fail to maintain the temporal dynamics of multivariate data, leading to degraded fidelity and performance degradation in forecasting tasks.

Method: The authors propose a model-agnostic MCMC framework that enforces consistency with empirical temporal transition statistics, thereby correcting deviations in conditional generative models during sequential data generation.

Result: Experiments on diverse datasets show significant improvement in metrics such as autocorrelation alignment, skewness error, kurtosis error, R$^2$, discriminative score, and predictive score using the MCMC framework.

Conclusion: Explicit preservation of transition laws is crucial for generating high-fidelity synthetic time series, providing a more principled approach than relying purely on adversarial distribution matching methods.

Abstract: Time-series data augmentation plays a crucial role in regression-oriented forecasting tasks, where limited data restricts the performance of deep learning models. While Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) have shown promise in synthetic time-series generation, existing approaches primarily focus on matching marginal data distributions and often overlook the temporal dynamics that naturally exist in the original multivariate time series. When generating multivariate time series, this mismatch leads to distribution shift and temporal drift, thereby degrading the fidelity of the synthetic sequences.
  In this work, we propose a model-agnostic Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)-based framework to mitigate distribution shift and preserve temporal dynamics in synthetic time series. We provide a theoretical analysis of how conditional generative models accumulate deviations under sequential generation and demonstrate that the MCMC algorithm can correct these discrepancies by enforcing consistency with empirical transition statistics between neighboring time points.
  Extensive experiments on the Lorenz, Licor, ETTh, and ILI datasets using RCGAN, GCWGAN, TimeGAN, SigCWGAN, and AECGAN demonstrate that the proposed MCMC framework consistently improves autocorrelation alignment, skewness error, kurtosis error, R$^2$, discriminative score, and predictive score. These results suggest that synthetic time series consistent with the original data require explicit preservation of transition laws rather than solely relying on adversarial distribution matching, thereby offering a principled direction for improving generative modeling of time-series data.

</details>


### [294] [Remaining Useful Life Estimation for Turbofan Engines: A Comparative Study of Classical, CNN, and LSTM Approaches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27234)
*Astitva Goel,Samarth Galchar,Sumit Kanu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper compares machine learning methods for predicting Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of turbofan engines using the NASA C-MAPSS dataset, with models including LSTM, CNN, and XGBoost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The aim is to provide proactive maintenance in industrial systems by accurately estimating equipment RUL using various machine learning methods.

Method: A comparative analysis of classical methods (e.g., Ridge Regression, Polynomial Ridge, XGBoost) and deep learning methods (1D CNN & LSTM) is performed, ensuring fair evaluation through consistent preprocessing pipelines.

Result: LSTM outperformed alternative methods with RMSE values of 14.93 for FD001 and 14.20 for FD003; XGBoost proved competitive with RMSE of 13.36 on FD003 segment.

Conclusion: LSTM offers simplicity and accuracy for RUL prediction, while XGBoost excels in nonlinear modeling, showing the value of diverse approaches for PHM tasks.

Abstract: Remaining Useful Life (RUL) estimation is a critical component of Prognostics and Health Management (PHM), enabling proactive maintenance scheduling and reducing unplanned failures in industrial equipment. This paper presents a comparative study of machine learning approaches for RUL estimation on the NASA C-MAPSS turbofan engine dataset: classical baselines (Ridge Regression, Polynomial Ridge, and XGBoost), a 1D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network.
