All models are evaluated on the FD001 and FD003 subsets under an identical preprocessing pipeline to ensure a fair comparison. Among raw-sequence models, the LSTM achieves RMSE of 14.93 and 14.20 on FD001 and FD003 respectively, outperforming the deep LSTM reported by Zheng et al.~\cite{paper} (RMSE 16.14 and 16.18) despite using a simpler single-layer architecture. The 1D CNN achieves RMSE of 16.97 on FD001 and 15.68 on FD003, demonstrating competitive performance on FD003 while producing more conservative RUL predictions on FD001. Ridge Regression is evaluated on raw and engineered features, while other classical models use only engineered inputs. XGBoost achieves an RMSE of 13.36 on FD003, highlighting the competitiveness of nonlinear modeling.

</details>


### [295] [Analytical Correction for Subsampling Bias in Drifting Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27239)
*Jiaru Zhang,Zeyun Deng,Juanwu Lu,Ziran Wang,Ruqi Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Drifting models use generative training by following a drifting field, but minibatch centroids introduce bias. The researchers propose Analytical Bias Correction (ABC) to address this issue, reducing bias effectively with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address bias in minibatch centroid estimations due to softmax self-normalization, as this bias impacts the performance of generative models that follow a drifting field.

Method: They introduce Analytical Bias Correction (ABC), a closed-form adjustment to approximate and correct the leading term of minibatch centroid bias without extra significant computational cost.

Result: ABC reduces the bias from $O(1/n)$ to $O(1/n^2)$, does not increase variance, and leads to lower Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) and faster training on datasets like CIFAR-10.

Conclusion: ABC is an efficient and practical solution for reducing minibatch centroid bias in generative model training, with proven effectiveness both theoretically and empirically.

Abstract: Drifting models are capable one-step generative models trained to follow a drifting field. The field combines attractive and repulsive softmax-weighted centroids over the data and current-generator distributions. In practice, only a minibatch of $n$ samples from each distribution is available, and each centroid is approximated by an empirical estimate. In this paper, we begin by showing that the minibatch centroid is in general a biased estimator of the target centroid, with a pointwise $O(1/n)$ bias arising from softmax self-normalization. Correcting this bias requires the expectation over the full distribution, which is intractable. We instead approximate the leading bias term from in-batch statistics and propose Analytical Bias Correction (ABC), a closed-form plug-in adjustment. We prove that ABC reduces the bias from $O(1/n)$ to $O(1/n^2)$, introduces no first-order increase in total variance, and preserves convex-hull containment of the corrected centroid. In practice, ABC requires only two additional lines of code and has negligible wall-time overhead under compiled execution. Toy experiments confirm the theoretical $O(1/n)$ and $O(1/n^2)$ scaling. On CIFAR-10, ABC reduces FID and trains faster, with the largest gains at small $n$, where the bias is most significant.

</details>


### [296] [AutoREC: A software platform for developing reinforcement learning agents for equivalent circuit model generation from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27266)
*Ali Jaberi,Yonatan Kurniawan,Robert Black,Shayan Mousavi M.,Kabir Verma,Zoya Sadighi,Santiago Miret,Jason Hattrick-Simpers*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AutoREC is a Python tool for creating equivalent circuit models (ECMs) from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data using reinforcement learning. It achieves high success rates, demonstrating scalability and adaptability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ECM generation from EIS data is heavily reliant on manual methods, which require domain expertise and limit scalability. Automation is needed to integrate ECM generation into self-driving laboratories and autonomous pipelines.

Method: The authors reformulate ECM construction as a Markov Decision Process and use a Double Deep Q-Network with prioritized experience replay. A dead-loop mitigation strategy ensures efficient exploration of the action space.

Result: The RL agent trained with AutoREC achieved over 99.6% success on synthetic datasets and generalized well to unseen experimental EIS data from diverse systems.

Conclusion: AutoREC demonstrates strong promise for scalable, adaptive, and automated ECM generation, making it suitable for autonomous workflows in electrochemistry.

Abstract: This paper introduces AutoREC, an open-source Python package for developing reinforcement learning (RL) agents to automatically generate equivalent circuit models (ECMs) from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data. While ECMs are a standard framework for interpreting EIS data, traditional identification is typically based on manual trial-and-error, which requires domain experts and limits scalability, particularly in autonomous experimental pipelines such as self-driving laboratories. AutoREC addresses this challenge by formulating ECM construction as a sequential decision-making problem within a Markov Decision Process framework. It implements a Double Deep Q-Network with prioritized experience replay, along with a dedicated dead-loop mitigation strategy, to efficiently explore a complex action space for circuit generation. To demonstrate the capabilities of the platform, we trained an RL agent using AutoREC and evaluated its strengths and limitations across diverse datasets, while also discussing possible strategies to mitigate these limitations in future agent designs. The trained agent achieved a success rate exceeding $99.6\%$ on synthetic datasets and demonstrated strong generalization to unseen experimental EIS data from batteries, corrosion, oxygen evolution reaction, and CO$_2$ reduction systems. These results position AutoREC as a promising platform for adaptive and data-driven ECM generation, with potential for integration into automated electrochemical workflows.

</details>


### [297] [BrainDINO: A Brain MRI Foundation Model for Generalizable Clinical Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27277)
*Yizhou Wu,Shansong Wang,Yuheng Li,Mojtaba Safari,Mingzhe Hu,Chih-Wei Chang,Harini Veeraraghavan,Xiaofeng Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces BrainDINO, a self-supervised learning model for brain MRI tasks, trained on 6.6 million unlabeled MRI images to deliver a scalable representation for diverse applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for analyzing brain MRI require task-specific designs and substantial labeled data, limiting their generalizability and scalability.

Method: The authors developed BrainDINO, a self-distilled foundation model trained on unlabeled axial MRI slices from 20 datasets, and evaluated its performance across various neuroimaging tasks using frozen encoders and lightweight task heads.

Result: BrainDINO outperformed or matched natural-image and MRI-specific baselines, particularly under limited label availability, supporting tasks like tumor segmentation, classification, and survival modeling.

Conclusion: Large-scale self-supervised representation learning can efficiently and robustly generalize to diverse brain MRI tasks, offering a scalable approach without the need for full-network fine-tuning or volumetric pretraining.

Abstract: Brain MRI underpins a wide range of neuroscientific and clinical applications, yet most learning-based methods remain task-specific and require substantial labeled data. Here we show that a single self-supervised representation can generalize across heterogeneous brain MRI endpoints. We trained BrainDINO, a self-distilled foundation model, on approximately 6.6 million unlabeled axial slices from 20 datasets encompassing broad variation in population, disease, and acquisition setting. Using a frozen encoder with lightweight task heads, BrainDINO supported transfer across tumor segmentation, neurodegenerative and neurodevelopmental conditions classification, brain age estimation, post-stroke temporal prediction, molecular status prediction, MRI sequence classification, and survival modeling. Across tasks and supervision regimes, BrainDINO consistently equaled or exceeded natural-image and MRI-specific self-supervised baselines, with particularly strong advantages under label scarcity. Representation analyses further showed anatomically organized and pathology-sensitive feature structure in the absence of task-specific supervision. Our findings indicate that large-scale slice-wise self-supervised learning can yield a unified brain MRI representation that supports diverse neuroimaging tasks without volumetric pretraining or full-network fine-tuning, establishing a scalable foundation for robust and data-efficient brain imaging analysis.

</details>


### [298] [Predicting Covariate-Driven Spatial Deformation for Nonstationary Gaussian Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27280)
*Minghao Gu,Weizhi Lin,Qiang Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel method linking spatial deformations and covariates for predictive modeling of nonstationary Gaussian Processes, addressing challenges in high-order interactions and limited samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance modeling and prediction of nonstationary GPs under varying covariate conditions, overcoming limitations of static spatial deformation approaches.

Method: Spatial deformations are modeled using velocity fields in a Lie algebra, reducing high-order interaction complexities and enabling concise functional forms with efficient estimation algorithms.

Result: The proposed method improves nonstationary GP predictions with limited covariate-deformation data, validated through simulations and case studies in manufacturing and geostatistics.

Conclusion: This approach expands the applicability of spatial deformation methodologies by incorporating covariate influence, offering practical solutions for complex spatial data modeling challenges.

Abstract: Nonstationary Gaussian processes (GPs) are essential for modeling complex, locally heterogeneous spatial data. A common modeling approach is the spatial deformation method that warps the domain to recover isotropy. However, this static method does not account for changes in spatial correlation induced by covariates, limiting its ability to predict nonstationary GPs under new covariate conditions.
  To enable predictive modeling of the deformation method, we propose to model the spatial deformation as a function of covariates. The spaces of diffeomorphic deformations and Euclidean covariate vectors are connected by characterizing deformations as generated by velocity fields living in a Lie algebra. To overcome the estimation instability caused by high-order interactions between multiple covariates in a general Lie algebra, we prove that those interactions can be truncated with a moderate physical assumption. Based on the theoretical results, a concise functional form of deformations driven by multiple covariates can be established, and an efficient estimation-inference algorithm is developed for out-of-sample nonstationary GP prediction with limited covariate-deformation sample pairs. The effectiveness and generalizability of the method are demonstrated on a simulation study and two case studies, in the fields of manufacturing and geostatistics, respectively.

</details>


### [299] [BoostLoRA: Growing Effective Rank by Boosting Adapters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27308)
*Raviteja Anantha,Nick Levato,Layne C. Price*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BoostLoRA is a gradient-boosting framework for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, overcoming performance limits by iteratively training and merging ultra-low-parameter adapters in separate subspaces, achieving high results across tasks without inference overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PEFT methods struggle with balancing adapter size and expressivity, as smaller adapters are confined to fixed low-rank subspaces, limiting their performance even with extended training.

Method: BoostLoRA uses a gradient-boosting framework that progressively trains minimal, ultra-low-parameter adapters and merges them after training. A ROTATE SVD basis strategy assigns each training round to a distinct orthogonal subspace, enabling the cumulative effective rank to grow linearly.

Result: BoostLoRA achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple domains, including 89.1% on GSM8K, 68.8% on MATH-500, 80.4% on HumanEval, and 57.2% on MBPP, surpassing both existing ultra-low-parameter approaches and full fine-tuning methods.

Conclusion: BoostLoRA is the first PEFT method that decouples per-round parameter cost from total representational capacity by enabling effective rank growth through iterative training. This innovation significantly enhances PEFT performance without increasing inference overhead.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods face a tradeoff between adapter size and expressivity: ultra-low-parameter adapters are confined to fixed low-rank subspaces, capping performance even with extended training. We propose BoostLoRA, a gradient-boosting framework that overcomes this limit by iteratively training and merging minimal adapters on the examples the current model gets wrong. A ROTATE SVD basis strategy assigns each round to an orthogonal subspace, so cumulative effective rank grows linearly with the number of rounds while each adapter remains ultra-low-rank. After merging, adapters are discarded, leaving zero inference overhead. On Qwen2.5-3B, BoostLoRA reaches 89.1% on GSM8K and 68.8% on MATH-500, surpassing both the best single-shot ultra-low parameter adapter (TinyLoRA) and full fine-tuning; on code generation it reaches 57.2% on MBPP and 80.4% on HumanEval while full fine-tuning drops below the zero-shot baseline. We also demonstrate cross-architecture transfer on protein binding classification with ESM2-650M and cross-entropy training. BoostLoRA is, to our knowledge, the first PEFT method whose effective rank grows with training, separating per-round parameter cost from total representational capacity.

</details>


### [300] [PINN-Cast: Exploring the Role of Continuous-Depth NODE in Transformers and Physics Informed Loss as Soft Physical Constraints in Short-term Weather Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27313)
*Hira Saleem,Flora Salim,Cormac Purcell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a weather forecasting approach that combines transformer models with Neural ODE dynamics and physics-informed objectives, aiming for improved efficiency and accuracy compared to traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Weather forecasting relies heavily on computationally expensive physics-based models, but there's a need for data-driven methods that incorporate physical constraints while efficiently handling smooth latent dynamics.

Method: The authors proposed a continuous-depth transformer encoder using Neural ODE dynamics for smooth representation updates. Additionally, they included a two-branch attention mechanism and a physics-informed training objective for improved accuracy.

Result: Their results demonstrated that the proposed model outperformed a standard transformer and a Neural ODE forecasting alternative in short-term weather forecasting tasks.

Conclusion: The combination of transformer models, Neural ODE dynamics, and physics-informed objectives offers a promising direction for weather forecasting, increasing efficiency and integrating physical principles effectively.

Abstract: Operational weather prediction has long relied on physics-based numerical weather prediction (NWP), whose accuracy comes at the cost of substantial compute and complex simulation workflows. Recent transformer-based forecasters offer efficient data-driven alternatives, however transformers are physics-agnostic models. Additionally, standard transformer encoders evolve representations through discrete layer updates that may be less suited to modeling smooth latent dynamics. In this work, we propose a continuous-depth transformer encoder for weather forecasting that integrates Neural Ordinary Differential Equation (Neural ODE) dynamics within each encoder block. Specifically, we replace discrete residual updates with ODE-based updates solved using adaptive numerical integration. We also introduce a two-branch attention module that combines conventional patch-wise self-attention with an auxiliary branch that applies a derivative operator to attention logits, providing an additional change-sensitive interaction signal. To further align forecasts with governing principles, we propose a customized physics-informed training objective that enforces physical consistency as a soft constraint. We evaluate the proposed method against a standard discrete transformer baseline and an existing continuous-time Neural ODE forecasting variant, demonstrating the importance of PINN-Cast in short term weather forecasting.

</details>


### [301] [A Short Note on Batch-efficient Divide-and-Conquer Algorithm for EigenDecomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27325)
*Yue Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a faster Divide-and-Conquer EigenDecomposition (ED) algorithm for batched matrices with dimensions smaller than 64.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The high computation cost of EigenDecomposition, especially in deep neural networks with mini-batch matrices, limits its usability in computer vision tasks.

Method: A batch-efficient Divide-and-Conquer based ED algorithm is proposed, aimed at handling larger matrices compared to their previous QR-based ED approach.

Result: The proposed method demonstrates substantial speed improvement over the Pytorch SVD function for matrices smaller than dimension 64.

Conclusion: The new approach successfully accelerates ED calculations, making it advantageous for batched scenarios with larger matrices while retaining numerical efficiency.

Abstract: EigenDecomposition (ED) is at the heart of many computer vision algorithms and applications. One crucial bottleneck limiting its usage is the expensive computation cost, particularly for a mini-batch of matrices in deep neural networks. Our previous work proposed a dedicated QR-based ED algorithm for batched small matrices (dim${<}32$). This short paper targets the limitation and proposes a batch-efficient Divide-and-Conquer based ED algorithm for larger matrices. The numerical test shows that for a mini-batch of matrices whose dimensions are smaller than $64$, our method can be much faster than the Pytorch SVD function.

</details>


### [302] [TypeBandit: Type-Level Context Allocation and Reweighting for Effective Attribute Completion in Heterogeneous Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27356)
*Ta-Yang Wang,Rajgopal Kannan,Viktor Prasanna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces TypeBandit, a methodology for addressing missing node attributes in heterogeneous graphs by leveraging type-level information asymmetry and a bandit-based sampling strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation lies in addressing the challenge of missing node attributes in heterogeneous graphs, particularly when different node types provide varying levels of useful information (type-dependent information asymmetry).

Method: TypeBandit employs topology-aware initialization, type-level bandit sampling, and joint representation learning. It allocates sampling resources across node types and generates type summaries that act as contextual signals. The method is applied as a front-end to existing heterogeneous GNNs.

Result: TypeBandit shows meaningful gains across datasets (DBLP, IMDB, ACM) under fixed-split protocols. Additional experiments validate its performance, stability, efficiency, and semantic propagation capabilities.

Conclusion: TypeBandit is a lightweight, practical, and flexible approach for heterogeneous attribute completion, especially where type-specific information is unevenly distributed and sampling resources are constrained.

Abstract: Heterogeneous graphs are widely used to model multi-relational systems, but missing node attributes remain a major bottleneck for downstream learning. In this paper, we identify and formalize type-dependent information asymmetry: the phenomenon that different node types provide substantially different levels of useful signal for attribute completion. Motivated by this observation, we propose TypeBandit, a lightweight, model-agnostic methodology for heterogeneous attribute completion. TypeBandit combines topology-aware initialization, type-level bandit sampling, and joint representation learning. It allocates a finite global sampling budget across node types, samples representative nodes within each type, and uses the resulting sampled type summaries as shared contextual signals during representation construction. By operating at the type level rather than over each target node's local neighborhood, TypeBandit keeps the adaptive state compact and practical for large heterogeneous graphs.
  A key advantage of TypeBandit is architectural flexibility. Rather than requiring a new heterogeneous graph neural network architecture, TypeBandit acts as a type-aware front end for representative heterogeneous GNN backbones, including R-GCN, HetGNN, HGT, and SimpleHGN. We further introduce a hybrid pretraining scheme that combines structural degree priors with feature propagation, yielding a more reliable initializer than degree-only pretraining. Under a fixed-split protocol on DBLP, IMDB, and ACM, TypeBandit provides dataset-dependent but practically meaningful gains. Additional ablation, stability, efficiency, semantic-propagation, and sampled OGBN-MAG experiments support TypeBandit as a practical strategy for heterogeneous attribute completion when type-specific information is unevenly distributed and sampling resources are limited.

</details>


### [303] [AG-TAL: Anatomically-Guided Topology-Aware Loss for Multiclass Segmentation of the Circle of Willis Using Large-Scale Multi-Center Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27357)
*Jialu Liu,Yue Cui,Shan Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces AG-TAL, a novel loss function tailored for multiclass segmentation of the Circle of Willis (CoW), improving segmentation accuracy and connectivity while addressing limitations in existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improving accuracy and addressing challenges in multiclass segmentation of the Circle of Willis (CoW), crucial for neurovascular disease diagnosis and management.

Method: The authors developed AG-TAL by combining anatomical priors and topology-aware loss modules: radius-aware Dice loss, breakage-aware clDice loss, and adjacency-aware co-occurrence loss. Additionally, they introduced a multi-center CoW dataset with unified annotations.

Result: AG-TAL achieved state-of-the-art segmentation accuracy for CoW arteries. It improved Dice scores across multiple datasets, particularly enhancing small artery segmentation performance compared to current methods.

Conclusion: AG-TAL demonstrates robust generalization and reliability for multiclass CoW segmentation, paving the way for clinical applications like morphological biomarker discovery in neurovascular diseases such as Alzheimer's.

Abstract: Accurate multiclass segmentation of the Circle of Willis (CoW) is essential for neurovascular disease management but remains challenging due to complex vascular topology and variable morphology. Existing deep learning methods often suffer from vascular discontinuities and inter-class misclassification, while current topological loss functions incur prohibitive computational costs in 3D multiclass settings. To address these limitations, we propose an Anatomically-Guided Topology-Aware Loss (AG-TAL) and introduce a large-scale, multi-center CoW dataset with unified annotations to facilitate robust model training. AG-TAL specifically integrates a radius-aware Dice loss to address class imbalance in small vessels, a breakage-aware clDice loss that utilizes group convolutions to efficiently preserve local connectivity, and an adjacency-aware co-occurrence loss that leverages anatomical priors to enforce distinct boundaries between neighboring arteries. Evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation, AG-TAL achieved an average Dice score of 80.85% for all CoW arteries, with small arteries notably higher by 1.05-3.09% compared to state-of-the-art methods. Across six independent datasets, the performance of AG-TAL achieved Dice scores ranging from 74.46% to 81.17% for all CoW arteries, with improvements of 2.20% to 9.98% for small arteries compared to other methods. This study demonstrates the superiority of AG-TAL in identifying multiclass CoW arteries and its ability to generalize well to multiple independent datasets. Furthermore, reliability analyses and clinical applications in an Alzheimer's disease cohort validate the AG-TAL's robustness and its potential for discovering imaging-based morphological biomarkers.

</details>


### [304] [Stable but Wrong: An Inference Limit in Galactic Archaeology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27368)
*Zhipeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The study highlights systematic biases in stellar age inferences within Galactic archaeology, especially in observational quality parameters, impacting the reliability of conclusions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To examine whether observational quality systematically biases age inferences in Galactic archaeology, challenging the assumption that better data always lead to more accurate interpretations.

Method: Analyzed a large sample of subgiant stars, specifically focusing on signal-to-noise ratio and parallax precision, and compared results to an independent asteroseismic reference.

Result: Found a parameter region where quality metrics caused systematic biases, yielding age formation timescales 0.5-1 Gyr off from the reference, despite appearing statistically reliable.

Conclusion: Observational biases can create stable yet incorrect results in stellar age estimations, calling for caution and refined methodologies in Galactic archaeology studies.

Abstract: Statistical inference in observational science typically relies on a fundamental assumption: as sample size increases and uncertainties decrease, the inferred results should converge to the true physical quantities. This assumption underpins the notion that big data lead to more reliable conclusions. In Galactic archaeology, stellar ages inferred from spectroscopic surveys are widely used to reconstruct the formation history of the Milky Way disk. The age metallicity relation (AMR) and its derived formation timescale are often regarded as key physical diagnostics of early disk evolution. This interpretation carries an implicit premise: that observational quality does not introduce systematic bias into age inference. Here we show that this premise may fail. Using a large sample of subgiant stars, we identify a region in the observational quality parameter space (signal-to-noise ratio and parallax precision) where the inferred formation timescale exhibits a systematic offset of 0.5-1 Gyr relative to an independent asteroseismic reference, while the statistical uncertainties remain small, thus producing a stable-but-wrong inference state.

</details>


### [305] [Detecting is Easy, Adapting is Hard: Local Expert Growth for Visual Model-Based Reinforcement Learning under Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27411)
*Haiyang Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper tackles the challenge of addressing distribution shifts in visual model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL), proposing a method called JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth that improves out-of-distribution (OOD) performance without harming in-distribution (ID) results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the robustness of visual MBRL agents in handling test environments with distribution shifts, as current approaches often fail to apply effective action-level corrections.

Method: The authors introduce JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth, which uses a frozen JEPA representation for shift recognition and adds local residual corrections via cluster-specific experts while keeping the baseline controller unchanged.

Result: The proposed method demonstrates statistically significant OOD performance improvements across four shift conditions, preserves ID performance, and offers incremental knowledge growth without needing repeated retraining.

Conclusion: Effective adaptation to distribution shifts in visual MBRL requires precise local action corrections, and JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth offers a promising direction for incremental learning and robust control under shifting environments.

Abstract: Visual model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) agents can perform well on the training distribution, but often break down once the test environment shifts. In visual MBRL, recognizing that a shift has occurred is often the easier part; the harder part is turning that recognition into useful action-level correction. We study several ways of responding to shift, including planning penalties, direct fine-tuning, global residual correction, and coarse gating. In our experiments, these approaches either do not improve closed-loop control or hurt in-distribution (ID) performance. Based on these negative results, we propose JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth. The method uses a frozen JEPA representation only for problem indexing, while cluster-specific residual experts add local action corrections on top of the original controller. The baseline controller itself is not modified. Using paired-bootstrap evaluation, we find that the original naive-preference variant is not stable under stricter testing. In contrast, the harder-pair variant produces statistically significant OOD improvements on all four evaluated shift conditions while preserving ID performance. The learned experts also remain useful when the same shift is encountered again, which supports the view of adaptation as incremental knowledge growth rather than repeated full retraining. We further show that automatic ID rejection can be achieved with simple density models, whereas fine-grained discrimination among OOD sub-families is limited by the representation. Overall, the results indicate that, for visual MBRL under distribution shift, the main challenge is not simply noticing that the environment has changed, but applying the right local action correction after the change has been recognized.

</details>


### [306] [ChipLingo: A Systematic Training Framework for Large Language Models in EDA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27415)
*Lei Li,Xingwen Yu,Jianguo Ni,Junxuan Zhu,Jieqiong Zhang,Jian Zhao,Zhi Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ChipLingo is a systematic pipeline to adapt language models for Electronic Design Automation (EDA), overcoming domain-specific challenges and delivering improved performance on a newly created EDA benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EDA requires specialized domain knowledge, and general large language models struggle due to challenges like limited expertise, tool confusion, and degraded performance after domain-specific training.

Method: ChipLingo uses a three-stage pipeline: constructing a domain corpus, domain-adaptive pretraining with different strategies, and instruction alignment using retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). It also introduces a unique EDA benchmark, EDA-Bench.

Result: ChipLingo-8B achieves 59.7% accuracy on EDA-Bench, outperforming same-scale models, while ChipLingo-32B reaches 70.02%, nearing top commercial models. Key findings include the effectiveness of QA augmentation and specific training strategies for performance improvement.

Conclusion: Systematic domain training like ChipLingo provides significant advancements for EDA tasks, showcasing the importance of targeted approaches for knowledge-intensive fields.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of semiconductor technology, Electronic Design Automation (EDA) has become an increasingly knowledge-intensive and document-driven engineering domain. Although large language models (LLMs) have shown strong general capabilities, applying them directly to EDA remains challenging due to limited domain expertise, cross-tool knowledge confusion, and degraded retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) performance after domain training. To address these issues, this paper presents ChipLingo, a systematic training pipeline for domain-adapted LLMs tailored to EDA scenarios.
  ChipLingo consists of three stages: domain corpus construction with multi-source data curation and QA augmentation, domain-adaptive pretraining with comparisons of different parameter training strategies, and instruction alignment with RAG scenario training under diverse retrieval conditions. We also curate an internal benchmark, EDA-Bench, covering representative EDA tool scenarios, with plans for public release.
  Experiments show that ChipLingo-8B achieves 59.7% accuracy on EDA-Bench, outperforming the same-scale base model and some larger general-purpose models. ChipLingo-32B reaches 70.02%, approaching leading closed-source commercial models. Further analysis shows that QA augmentation improves domain performance, Partial FT offers a better balance between adaptation and general capability retention than LoRA, and explicit RAG scenario training mitigates the decline in retrieval utilization after domain training. These results demonstrate the practical value of systematic domain training for knowledge-intensive EDA tasks and provide a foundation for future EDA agents and external-knowledge-driven systems.

</details>


### [307] [AdaBFL: Multi-Layer Defensive Adaptive Aggregation for Bzantine-Robust Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27434)
*Zehui Tang,Yuchen Liu,Feihu Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes AdaBFL, a multi-layer defensive adaptive aggregation method for federated learning, enhancing resistance to varied Byzantine attacks without requiring server-side datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning is susceptible to poisoning attacks by malicious clients, which existing methods fail to address comprehensively without server dataset reliance.

Method: Introduces AdaBFL, a three-layer defense mechanism that adaptively adjusts weights of algorithms to tackle a range of attacks and ensures robustness in non-convex settings with non-iid data.

Result: Experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate AdaBFL's superior performance compared to other algorithms in handling Byzantine attacks.

Conclusion: AdaBFL effectively strengthens federated learning's resilience to complex attacks, proving convergence properties and achieving robust defense without dataset dependence at the server.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) is a popular distributed learning paradigm in machine learning, which enables multiple clients to collaboratively train models under the guidance of a server without exposing private client data. However, FL's decentralized nature makes it vulnerable to poisoning attacks, where malicious clients can submit corrupted models to manipulate the system. To counter such attacks, although various Byzantine-robust methods have been proposed, these methods struggle to provide balanced defense against multiple types of attacks or rely on possessing the dataset in the server. To deal with these drawbacks, thus, we propose an effective multi-layer defensive adaptive aggregation for Bzantine-robust federated learning (AdaBFL) based on a novel three-layer defensive mechanism, which can adaptively adjust the weights of defense algorithms to counter complex attacks. Moreover, we provide convergence properties of our AdaBFL method under the non-convex setting on non-iid data. Comprehensive experiments across multiple datasets validate the superiority of our AdaBFL over the comparable algorithms.

</details>


### [308] [ABC: Any-Subset Autoregression via Non-Markovian Diffusion Bridges in Continuous Time and Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27443)
*Gabe Guo,Thanawat Sornwanee,Lutong Hao,Elon Litman,Stefano Ermon,Jose Blanchet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces ABC, a novel autoregressive model for generating continuous stochastic processes in time and space, addressing limitations of existing diffusion models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion models struggle with maintaining structural relationships between states close in time, unstable integration, and incorrect dynamics, and fail to condition on arbitrary subsets of states.

Method: The authors propose Any-Subset Autoregressive Models (ABC) based on Non-Markovian Diffusion Bridges, leveraging a single continual SDE for time and state alignment and path-/time-dependent denoising score matching for learning dynamics.

Result: ABC achieves better performance over competing methods on tasks such as video generation and weather forecasting.

Conclusion: ABC enhances modeling of continuous processes by incorporating time-adaptive noise, realistic dynamics, and flexible conditioning, demonstrating strong empirical results in diverse domains.

Abstract: Generating continuous-time, continuous-space stochastic processes (e.g., videos, weather forecasts) conditioned on partial observations (e.g., first and last frames) is a fundamental challenge. Existing approaches, (e.g., diffusion models), suffer from key limitations: (1) noise-to-data evolution fails to capture structural similarity between states close in physical time and has unstable integration in low-step regimes; (2) random noise injected is insensitive to the physical process's time elapsed, resulting in incorrect dynamics; (3) they overlook conditioning on arbitrary subsets of states (e.g., irregularly sampled timesteps, future observations). We propose ABC: Any-Subset Autoregressive Models via Non-Markovian Diffusion Bridges in Continuous Time and Space. Crucially, we model the process with one continual SDE whose time variable and intermediate states track the real time and process states. This has provable advantages: (1) the starting point for generating future states is the already-close previous state, rather than uninformative noise; (2) random noise injection scales with physical time elapsed, encouraging physically plausible dynamics with similar time-adjacent states. We derive SDE dynamics via changes-of-measure on path space, yielding another advantage: (3) path-dependent conditioning on arbitrary subsets of the state history and/or future. To learn these dynamics, we derive a path- and time-dependent extension of denoising score matching. Our experiments show ABC's superiority to competing methods on multiple domains, including video generation and weather forecasting.

</details>


### [309] [Improving Graph Few-shot Learning with Hyperbolic Space and Denoising Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27462)
*Yonghao Liu,Jialu Sun,Wei Pang,Fausto Giunchiglia,Ximing Li,Xiaoyue Feng,Renchu Guan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces IMPRESS, a novel framework leveraging hyperbolic space and denoising diffusion to improve graph few-shot learning, addressing key issues in existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle the limitations of existing graph few-shot learning methods that fail to capture hierarchical structures and rely on suboptimal support distributions.

Method: IMPRESS employs hyperbolic space for effective node representation learning and adopts denoising diffusion mechanisms to refine support distributions during the learning process.

Result: IMPRESS delivers tighter theoretical generalization bounds and demonstrates consistent performance improvements across various benchmark datasets compared to baselines.

Conclusion: Leveraging hyperbolic space and denoising diffusion, IMPRESS effectively enhances graph few-shot learning, making it better suited to handle real-world hierarchical graphs and limited support samples.

Abstract: Graph few-shot learning, which focuses on effectively learning from only a small number of labeled nodes to quickly adapt to new tasks, has garnered significant research attention. Despite recent advances in graph few-shot learning that have demonstrated promising performance, existing methods still suffer from several key limitations. First, during the meta-training phase, these methods typically perform node representation learning in Euclidean space, which often fails to capture the inherently hierarchical structure existing in real-world graph data. Second, during the meta-testing phase, they usually fit an empirical target distribution derived from only a few support samples, even when this distribution significantly deviates from the true underlying distribution. To address these issues, we propose IMPRESS, a novel framework that IMproves graPh few-shot learning with hypeRbolic spacE and denoiSing diffuSion. Specifically, our model learns node representations in a hyperbolic space and enriches the support distribution through denoising diffusion mechanisms. Theoretically, IMPRESS achieves a tighter generalization bound. Empirically, IMPRESS consistently outperforms competitive baselines across multiple benchmark datasets.

</details>


### [310] [Toward Scalable SDN for LEO Mega-Constellations: A Graph Learning Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27478)
*Sivaram Krishnan,Bassel Al Homssi,Zhouyou Gu,Jihong Park,Sung-Min Oh,Jinho Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hierarchical software-defined networking framework leveraging Graph Neural Networks and Koopman theory to enhance management of low Earth orbit satellite constellations, achieving improved spatial and temporal forecasting performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The limitations of terrestrial networks drive the need for integrating large-scale non-terrestrial networks such as LEO satellite constellations. Efficient management of these mega-constellations faces challenges due to their sheer scale.

Method: The paper introduces a hierarchical SDN framework that uses Graph Neural Networks for compact representation and Koopman theory for linearizing nonlinear dynamics. A Graph Koopman Autoencoder is utilized for spatio-temporal forecasting within a linear subspace for each orbital shell, centrally aggregated by an SDN controller for global control.

Result: Simulations on the Starlink constellation show a 42.8% improvement in spatial compression and a 10.81% improvement in temporal forecasting, while maintaining a significantly smaller model footprint compared to existing baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses scalability challenges in satellite network management, demonstrating promising improvements in efficiency and forecasting accuracy.

Abstract: Terrestrial network limitations drive the integration of non-terrestrial networks (NTNs), notably mega-constellations comprising thousands of low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites. While these satellites act as interconnected network switches via inter-satellite links (ISLs), their massive scale creates severe bottlenecks for network management. To address this, we propose a scalable, hierarchical software-defined networking (SDN) framework. Our architecture leverages graph neural networks (GNNs) to compactly represent the constellation topology, and Koopman theory to linearize nonlinear dynamics. Specifically, a Graph Koopman Autoencoder (GKAE) forecasts spatio-temporal behavior within a linear subspace for each orbital shell. A central SDN controller then aggregates these shell-level predictions for globally coordinated control. Simulations on the Starlink constellation demonstrate that our approach achieves at least a 42.8\% improvement in spatial compression and a 10.81\% improvement in temporal forecasting compared to established baselines, all while utilizing a significantly smaller model footprint.

</details>


### [311] [Low Rank Adaptation for Adversarial Perturbation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27487)
*Han Liu,Shanghao Shi,Yevgeniy Vorobeychik,Chongjie Zhang,Ning Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates the low-rank structure of adversarial perturbations and introduces a low-rank technique to enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of black-box adversarial attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation arises from the analogy between the optimization processes in model training and adversarial example generation, suggesting that adversarial perturbations might also exhibit a low-rank structure.

Method: The authors first identify low-rank structures in adversarial perturbations. They then propose a method to project gradients into a low-dimensional subspace using a reference model and auxiliary data, followed by confining perturbation searches within the subspace during black-box attacks.

Result: Empirical evaluations demonstrate that their low-rank adversarial technique improves both the efficiency and effectiveness of black-box adversarial attacks across various models and datasets.

Conclusion: The study confirms the inherently low-rank structure of adversarial perturbations and provides a framework leveraging this insight to significantly enhance adversarial attack strategies.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), which leverages the insight that model updates typically reside in a low-dimensional space, has significantly improved the training efficiency of Large Language Models (LLMs) by updating neural network layers using low-rank matrices. Since the generation of adversarial examples is an optimization process analogous to model training, this naturally raises the question: Do adversarial perturbations exhibit a similar low-rank structure?
  In this paper, we provide both theoretical analysis and extensive empirical investigation across various attack methods, model architectures, and datasets to show that adversarial perturbations indeed possess an inherently low-rank structure. This insight opens up new opportunities for improving both adversarial attacks and defenses. We mainly focus on leveraging this low-rank property to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of black-box adversarial attacks, which often suffer from excessive query requirements. Our method follows a two-step approach. First, we use a reference model and auxiliary data to guide the projection of gradients into a low-dimensional subspace. Next, we confine the perturbation search in black-box attacks to this low-rank subspace, significantly improving the efficiency and effectiveness of the adversarial attacks.
  We evaluated our approach across a range of attack methods, benchmark models, datasets, and threat models. The results demonstrate substantial and consistent improvements in the performance of our low-rank adversarial attacks compared to conventional methods.

</details>


### [312] [FMCL: Class-Aware Client Clustering with Foundation Model Representations for Heterogeneous Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27510)
*Mahad Ali,Laura J. Brattain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces FMCL, a one-shot class-aware client clustering framework for Federated Learning (FL) using foundation model representations to tackle statistical heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the performance degradation in Federated Learning under statistical heterogeneity by optimizing client clustering methods that capture semantic structures across clients.

Method: FMCL uses a frozen foundation model to generate class-level embedding prototypes, computes client similarities via cosine distance, and performs clustering prior to training without additional communication or dependencies on downstream models.

Result: Experiments show FMCL enhances federated performance, achieves more stable clustering, and outperforms existing clustering strategies in non-identical data setups.

Conclusion: FMCL proves effective in improving clustered Federated Learning through a class-aware, foundation model-driven approach, showcasing its ability to handle heterogeneity efficiently.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across distributed clients without sharing raw data, yet its performance deteriorates under statistical heterogeneity. Clustered Federated Learning addresses this challenge by grouping similar clients and training separate models per cluster. However, existing clustering strategies often rely on raw data statistics, model parameters, or heuristic similarity measures that fail to capture class-level semantic structure across heterogeneous domains and frequently require iterative coordination. We propose FMCL, a one-shot, class-aware client clustering framework that leverages foundation model representations to construct semantic client signatures. Using a frozen foundation model, FMCL computes class-level embedding prototypes for each client and measures similarity via cosine distance between their class-aware representations. Clustering is performed once prior to training, introducing no additional communication during federated optimization and remaining agnostic to the downstream model architecture. Extensive experiments across heterogeneous benchmarks demonstrate that FMCL improves federated performance and yields more stable clustering behavior compared to existing clustering-based methods under non-identically distributed data partitioning.

</details>


### [313] [Diagnosing Capability Gaps in Fine-Tuning Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27547)
*Saeid Asgari Taghanaki,Rakshanda Agarwal,Bruce Sun,Rohan Jha,Elias Stengel-Eskin,Sara Malvar,Rui Ying,Yifei Xu,Guilherme Potje,Tusher Chakraborty,Leonardo de Oliveira Nunes,Ranveer Chandra,Emre Kiciman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces GoalCover, a framework that identifies missing capabilities in datasets used for fine-tuning large language models, validating its effectiveness through experiments and improvements in downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) for specific domains requires datasets that thoroughly cover desired capabilities, but assessing gaps in such datasets before fine-tuning is challenging.

Method: GoalCover employs interactive goal decomposition, automated coverage analysis, and LLM alignment scoring to identify dataset gaps, along with testing using structured experiments and practical downstream applications.

Result: The framework reliably identifies capability gaps, proven through controlled experiments across domains and real-world tasks. For instance, it improved performance metrics in a financial summarization task by filtering and augmenting training data.

Conclusion: GoalCover is an effective diagnostic tool for detecting dataset deficiencies prior to fine-tuning, offering actionable insights for improving training data coverage and enhancing model performance.

Abstract: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) for domain-specific tasks requires training datasets that comprehensively cover the target capabilities a practitioner needs. Yet identifying which capabilities a dataset fails to support, and doing so before an expensive fine-tuning run, remains a largely unsolved problem. We introduce GoalCover, a framework that helps practitioners systematically detect capability gaps in fine-tuning datasets through interactive goal decomposition and automated coverage assessment. GoalCover guides a practitioner through structured decomposition of a high-level goal into atomic, independently evaluable subgoals; assigns each training sample an LLM-based alignment score against every subgoal; and surfaces missing capabilities through automated analysis of low-scoring sample explanations. We validate the framework along two complementary axes. First, through controlled corruption experiments across three domains (medical QA, legal summarization, code generation), we show that GoalCover reliably distinguishes targeted from non-targeted capability impacts: target subgoals degrade by 25.6% on average versus 2.1% for non-target subgoals (Cohen's d=1.24). Second, we demonstrate downstream utility on a financial-summarization Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) task with Qwen-3-14B: training on GoalCover-filtered data improves the LLM-judge reward from 3.77 to 4.12 (out of 5) over the unfiltered baseline, and combining filtered data with goal-conditioned synthetic samples yields the strongest result (4.20). The two results together show that GoalCover works as a practical pre-fine-tuning diagnostic: it detects capability gaps and produces concrete signal for closing them.

</details>


### [314] [Beyond the Training Distribution: Mapping Generalization Boundaries in Neural Program Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27551)
*Henrik Voigt,Michael Habeck,Joachim Giesen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes transformers in a controlled program synthesis environment to assess generalization versus memorization. The results reveal the importance of diverse training data for out-of-distribution generalization and highlight current limitations in scaling and extrapolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to disentangle true generalization from mere memorization in large-scale transformers used for program synthesis, addressing concerns about contaminated training data and lack of interpretability in existing benchmarks.

Method: They develop a controlled environment with a domain-specific arithmetic grammar and systematically evaluate programs with designed metric spaces, enabling precise data distribution mapping and tailored train-test splits.

Result: Experiments show that optimizing diversity in training data enhances out-of-distribution generalization, but transformers struggle with generating syntactically novel programs. Scaling compute provides generalization gains, but only in a log-linear fashion.

Conclusion: The study concludes that maximizing training diversity across various manifolds is crucial for robust generalization. New search-based methods are required to surpass the limitations of current scaling trends.

Abstract: Large-scale transformers achieve impressive results on program synthesis benchmarks, yet their true generalization capabilities remain obscured by data contamination and opaque training corpora. To rigorously assess whether models are truly generalizing or merely retrieving memorized templates, we introduce a strictly controlled program synthesis environment based on a domain-specific arithmetic grammar. By systematically enumerating and evaluating millions of unique programs, we construct interpretable syntactic and semantic metric spaces. This allows us to precisely map data distributions and sample train and test splits that isolate specific distributional shifts. Our experiments demonstrate that optimizing density generalization -- through diverse sampling over both semantic and syntactic spaces -- induces robust out-of-distribution generalization. Conversely, evaluating support generalization reveals that transformers severely struggle with extrapolation, experiencing a performance drop of over 30% when forced to generate syntactically novel programs. While steadily scaling up compute improves generalization, the gains follow a strictly log-linear relationship. We conclude that robust generalization requires maximizing training diversity across multiple manifolds, and our findings indicate the necessity for novel search-based approaches to break through current log-linear scaling bottlenecks.

</details>


### [315] [Online semi-supervised perception: Real-time learning without explicit feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27562)
*Branislav Kveton,Michal Valko,Matthai Phillipose,Ling Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a real-time learning algorithm that doesn't need explicit feedback, blending semi-supervised learning and online learning techniques for dynamic updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable learning algorithms to adapt in real time using minimal pre-labeled data and without requiring explicit feedback.

Method: The algorithm iteratively builds a graph-based representation of its environment, initialized with labeled data. It updates this representation using an online stream of unlabeled examples.

Result: The algorithm demonstrates superior precision and recall in real-time face recognition tasks on three challenging video datasets.

Conclusion: This approach proves effective for real-time learning tasks, providing an efficient and adaptive method for real-time face recognition and similar challenges.

Abstract: This paper proposes an algorithm for real-time learning without explicit feedback. The algorithm combines the ideas of semi-supervised learning on graphs and online learning. In particular, it iteratively builds a graphical representation of its world and updates it with observed examples. Labeled examples constitute the initial bias of the algorithm and are provided offline, and a stream of unlabeled examples is collected online to update this bias. We motivate the algorithm, discuss how to implement it efficiently, prove a regret bound on the quality of its solutions, and apply it to the problem of real-time face recognition. Our recognizer runs in real time, and achieves superior precision and recall on 3 challenging video datasets.

</details>


### [316] [Bayesian policy gradient and actor-critic algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27563)
*Mohammad Ghavamzadeh,Yaakov Engel,Michal Valko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Bayesian framework for policy gradient methods in reinforcement learning, addressing high variance and slow convergence issues by modeling the policy gradient as a Gaussian process and integrating with actor-critic learning using Gaussian process temporal difference learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional policy gradient methods suffer from high variance, requiring a large number of samples and leading to slow convergence. The authors aim to reduce sample requirements and improve convergence by introducing a Bayesian approach to policy gradient methods.

Method: The paper models policy gradient as a Gaussian process to reduce sample requirements, estimates natural gradients and their uncertainty, and integrates this with a Bayesian actor-critic model using Gaussian process temporal difference learning to model action-value functions with closed-form gradient computations.

Result: The experiments demonstrate that the proposed Bayesian policy gradient and actor-critic methods outperform traditional Monte-Carlo-based policy gradient methods on various reinforcement learning problems.

Conclusion: The Bayesian framework for policy gradients provides accurate gradient estimates with fewer samples and extends to partially observable problems. Despite limitations on exploiting Markov properties, the integration of Bayesian actor-critic models enhances reinforcement learning efficiency and effectiveness.

Abstract: Policy gradient methods are reinforcement learning algorithms that adapt a parameterized policy by following a performance gradient estimate. Conventional policy gradient methods use Monte-Carlo techniques to estimate the gradient, which tend to have high variance, requiring many samples and resulting in slow convergence. We first propose a Bayesian framework for policy gradient, based on modeling the policy gradient as a Gaussian process. This reduces the number of samples needed to obtain accurate gradient estimates. Moreover, estimates of the natural gradient and a measure of the uncertainty in the gradient estimates, namely, the gradient covariance, are provided at little extra cost. Since the proposed framework considers system trajectories as its basic observable unit, it does not require the dynamics within trajectories to be of any particular form, and can be extended to partially observable problems. On the downside, it cannot exploit the Markov property when the system is Markovian. To address this, we supplement our Bayesian policy gradient framework with a new actor-critic learning model in which a Bayesian class of non-parametric critics, based on Gaussian process temporal difference learning, is used. Such critics model the action-value function as a Gaussian process, allowing Bayes rule to be used to compute the posterior distribution over action-value functions, conditioned on the observed data. Appropriate choices of the policy parameterization and of the prior covariance (kernel) between action-values yield closed-form expressions for the posterior of the gradient of the expected return with respect to the policy parameters. We perform detailed experimental comparisons of the proposed Bayesian policy gradient and actor-critic algorithms with classic Monte-Carlo based policy gradient methods, on a number of reinforcement learning problems.

</details>


### [317] [Learning from a single labeled face and a stream of unlabeled data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27564)
*Branislav Kveton,Michal Valko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study addresses one-person face recognition from a single image using an innovative algorithm and unlabeled data, achieving a 90% recognition rate with minimal false positives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile and personal computer authentication lacks a solution for one-class face recognition settings, especially where only a single image (positive example) and no negative examples are available.

Method: The authors propose a non-parametric algorithm that learns from one labeled image of a face and utilizes abundant unlabeled data, formalizing the problem as one-class classification.

Result: Using a dataset of 43 individuals, the method achieved a 90% recognition rate with nearly zero false positives, outperforming the best baseline by over 25% in recall.

Conclusion: The proposed approach significantly advances face recognition in single-image-per-person settings, and a sensitivity analysis provides practical guidance for parameter tuning.

Abstract: Face recognition from a single image per person is a challenging problem because the training sample is extremely small. We consider a variation of this problem. In our problem, we recognize only one person, and there are no labeled data for any other person. This setting naturally arises in authentication on personal computers and mobile devices, and poses additional challenges because it lacks negative examples. We formalize our problem as one-class classification, and propose and analyze an algorithm that learns a non-parametric model of the face from a single labeled image and a stream of unlabeled data. In many domains, for instance when a person interacts with a computer with a camera, unlabeled data are abundant and easy to utilize. This is the first paper that investigates how these data can help in learning better models in the single-image-per-person setting. Our method is evaluated on a dataset of 43 people and we show that these people can be recognized 90% of time at nearly zero false positives. This recall is 25+% higher than the recall of our best performing baseline. Finally, we conduct a comprehensive sensitivity analysis of our algorithm and provide a guideline for setting its parameters in practice.

</details>


### [318] [Statistical Channel Fingerprint Construction for Massive MIMO: A Unified Tensor Learning Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27574)
*Zhenzhou Jin,Li You,Xiang-Gen Xia,Xiqi Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Statistical Channel Fingerprint (sCF) for channel state information in MIMO systems, developing a tensor-based learning model, LPWTNet, that excels in reconstruction accuracy and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enable efficient acquisition and reconstruction of statistical CSI (sCSI) for MIMO systems under practical constraints like cost, privacy, and security.

Method: The authors use a unified tensor-based learning approach, LPWTNet, incorporating Laplacian pyramid decomposition, wavelet transform-based convolution, and mask learning for refined feature extraction.

Result: Experiments demonstrate LPWTNet's superior reconstruction accuracy and computational efficiency compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: LPWTNet proves effective for sCF construction in varied scenarios, advancing sCSI acquisition in MIMO systems with practical advantages.

Abstract: Channel fingerprint (CF) is considered a key enabler for facilitating the acquisition of channel state information (CSI) in massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) communication systems. In this work, we investigate a novel type of CF that stores statistical CSI (sCSI) at each potential location, referred to as statistical CF (sCF). Specifically, we reveal the relationship between sCSI, namely the channel spatial covariance matrix (CSCM), and the channel power angular spectrum (CPAS). Building on this foundation, we construct a unified tensor representation of the sCF and further reduce its dimension by exploiting the eigenvalue decomposition of the CSCM and its correlation with the PAS. Considering the practical constraints imposed by measurement cost, privacy, and security, we focus on three representative scenarios and uniformly formulate them as tensor restoration tasks. To this end, we propose a unified tensor-based learning architecture, termed LPWTNet. The architecture incorporates a closed-form Laplacian pyramid (LP) decomposition and reconstruction framework that replaces the traditional encoder-decoder structure, enabling efficient inference while capturing multi-scale frequency subband characteristics of the sCF. Additionally, a shared mask learning strategy is introduced to adaptively refine high-frequency sCF components through level-wise adjustments. To achieve a larger receptive field without over-parameterization, we further propose a small-kernel convolution mechanism based on the wavelet transform (WT), which decouples convolution across different frequency components of the sCF and enhances feature extraction efficiency. Extensive experiments show that the proposed approach delivers competitive reconstruction accuracy and computational efficiency across various sCF construction scenarios when compared with state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [319] [Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning via Differential Privacy and Homomorphic Encryption for Cardiovascular Disease Risk Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27598)
*Gaurang Sharma,Juha Pajula,Aada Illikainen,Markus Rautell,Noora Lipsonen,Petri Alhainen,Mika Hilvo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The study evaluates the integration of Differential Privacy (DP) and Homomorphic Encryption (HE) in Federated Learning (FL) using Swedish healthcare data to understand the privacy-utility trade-offs and provide practical insights for real-world healthcare systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of protecting sensitive patient data while enabling collaborative analysis without centralizing data, and to assess the privacy-utility trade-offs of integrating Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs) like DP and HE into Federated Learning (FL).

Method: Conducted a systematic evaluation comparing Differential Privacy and Homomorphic Encryption within Federated Learning against standard FL and centralized Machine Learning (cML). Tested cardiovascular disease risk prediction using logistic regression (LR) and neural network (NN) models on Swedish healthcare data.

Result: FL with Homomorphic Encryption maintained model performance close to centralized Machine Learning but incurred additional cryptographic overhead, especially for neural networks. FL with Differential Privacy had lower computational demands but introduced performance degradation, with logistic regression more affected by noise than neural networks.

Conclusion: Integrating DP and HE can enhance the privacy of federated learning in healthcare, but each has unique trade-offs. HE showed better utility with higher computational cost, while DP incurred less overhead but greater sensitivity to noise. These findings help optimize privacy-preserving approaches in multi-institutional healthcare systems.

Abstract: Protecting sensitive health data while enabling collaborative analysis is a central challenge in healthcare. Traditional machine learning (ML) requires institutions to pool anonymized patient records, centralizing analytical development and privacy risks at a single site. Privacy-enhancing technologies (PETs), including Differential Privacy (DP) and Homomorphic Encryption (HE), can mitigate these risks. However, they are mainly studied in conventional data-sharing settings and often introduce trade-offs, including reduced model utility, higher computational cost, and increased implementation complexity. Federated Learning (FL) reduces data centralization by enabling institutions to train models locally and share only model updates. Nevertheless, FL does not eliminate privacy risks, as shared parameters or gradients may still reveal sensitive information. Integrating DP or HE into FL can strengthen privacy guarantees, yet their comparative performance and deployment implications in real-world healthcare settings remain unclear.
  We systematically evaluated DP and HE integration in FL under real-world conditions, comparing them with standard FL and centralized ML (cML) to quantify privacy-utility trade-offs in multi-institutional settings. Using nationwide Swedish healthcare data, we evaluated cardiovascular disease risk prediction using logistic regression (LR) and neural network (NN) learners. FL with HE achieved performance comparable to cML but introduced measurable cryptographic overhead, particularly in the NN implementation. FL with DP incurred lower computational cost; however, LR was more sensitive to calibrated noise than the NN, resulting in greater performance degradation. Our findings provide practical guidance for deploying privacy-preserving FL in fragmented healthcare systems.

</details>


### [320] [ZAYAN: Disentangled Contrastive Transformer for Tabular Remote Sensing Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27606)
*Al Zadid Sultan Bin Habib,Tanpia Tasnim,Md. Ekramul Islam,Muntasir Tabasum*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces ZAYAN, a self-supervised framework for tabular data, emphasizing feature-level contrastive learning to improve accuracy, robustness, and generalization, especially under label scarcity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address challenges in learning from tabular data, such as heterogeneity, lack of labels, and feature redundancy, particularly in remote sensing and environmental science contexts.

Method: The method is a two-module framework: ZAYAN-CL pretrains feature embeddings using a zero-anchor contrastive objective with dynamic perturbations and masking, while ZAYAN-T applies a Transformer conditioned on the feature embeddings for downstream tasks.

Result: ZAYAN demonstrated superior performance across eight datasets, including gains in accuracy, robustness, and generalization even under label scarcity and distribution shift conditions.

Conclusion: Feature-level contrastive learning combined with dynamic encoding provides an effective approach for handling tabular remote sensing data, outperforming traditional tabular deep learning methods.

Abstract: Learning informative representations from tabular data in remote sensing and environmental science is challenging due to heterogeneity, scarce labels, and redundancy among features. We present ZAYAN (Zero-Anchor dYnamic feAture eNcoding), a self-supervised, feature-centric contrastive framework for tabular data. ZAYAN performs contrastive learning at the feature rather than sample level, removing the need for explicit anchor selection and any reliance on class labels, while encouraging a redundancy-minimized, disentangled embedding space. The framework has two modules: ZAYAN-CL, which pretrains feature embeddings via a zero-anchor contrastive objective with dynamic perturbations and masking, and ZAYAN-T, a Transformer that conditions on these embeddings for downstream classification. Across eight datasets, including six remote-sensing tabular benchmarks and two remote-sensing-driven flood-prediction tables from satellite and GIS products, ZAYAN achieves superior accuracy, robustness, and generalization over tabular deep learning baselines, with consistent gains under label scarcity and distribution shift. These results indicate that feature-level contrastive learning and dynamic feature encoding provide an effective recipe for learning from tabular sensing data.

</details>


### [321] [AMGenC: Generating Charge Balanced Amorphous Materials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27613)
*Yan Lin,Jilin Hu,N. M. Anoop Krishnan,Morten M. Smedskjaer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces AMGenC, a generative design method for amorphous materials, ensuring charge-balanced designs with improved computational efficiency and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the challenge in designing amorphous materials with desired properties using generative inverse design methods, overcoming the charge imbalance issue common in such approaches.

Method: A novel generative inverse design method, AMGenC, is proposed. The method uses element noise initialization and combined soft and discrete projections to ensure charge balance throughout the generative process.

Result: AMGenC demonstrated success in generating charge balanced amorphous materials with minimal computational overhead and without losing inverse design accuracy. Results were validated with experiments on two datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed AMGenC method effectively overcomes the charge imbalance problem in generative inverse design of amorphous materials, providing a promising tool for materials design with better resource efficiency and reliability.

Abstract: Amorphous (disordered) materials are solids that have shown great potential in various domains, including energy storage, thermal management, and advanced materials. Unlike crystalline materials that can be described by unit cells containing a few to hundreds of atoms, amorphous materials require larger simulation cells with at least hundreds to thousands of atoms. To advance the design of amorphous materials with desired properties and facilitate the exploration of their vast design space, generative inverse design has emerged as a promising approach. It aims to directly output materials with properties closely aligned with the desired ones using probabilistic generative models conditioned on desired properties, which can be more resource efficient than the traditional trial-and-error approach. However, due to the inherent stochasticity of probabilistic generative models, when element assignments are unconstrained, a large portion of generated materials may be charge unbalanced, and no existing methods can effectively mitigate this limitation. In this work, we propose AMGenC, a new generative inverse design method for amorphous materials that can guarantee the generation of charge balanced samples, with minimal additional computational overhead and without sacrificing inverse design accuracy. AMGenC achieves this through an element noise that gives the generation process a starting point centered around charge balance, and the combination of a per-step soft projection and a final discrete projection for steering the elements toward exact charge balance throughout the generation. We perform extensive experiments on two amorphous materials datasets. Experimental results provide evidence that AMGenC achieves its design goal.

</details>


### [322] [Green Physics-Informed Machine Learning Models For Structural Health Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27638)
*Daisy R Bradley,Elizabeth J Cross*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper explores physics-informed machine learning (grey-box models) for structural health monitoring, comparing their environmental impact and computational costs to purely data-driven approaches (black-box models).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the limitations of purely data-driven machine learning models in applications lacking relevant environmental and operational data, proposing physics-informed models as a solution.

Method: The authors compare the performance and environmental impact of black-box and grey-box models, using a structural health monitoring case study and emphasizing reduced runtimes and carbon emissions in grey-box models.

Result: Physics-informed grey-box models demonstrate superior extrapolative performance, leading to reduced runtimes and carbon emissions compared to black-box models.

Conclusion: Integrating physical insights into machine learning enhances efficiency, reduces environmental impact, and sustains performance in structural engineering applications.

Abstract: Machine learning continues to emerge as an important tool to be utilised within structural engineering and structural health monitoring, due to its ability to accurately and quickly perform both regression and classification tasks. However, a purely data driven approach has its limitations, particularly where we lack data from relevant environmental and operational conditions, a situation that has led to the development of physics-informed machine learners for structural health monitoring. These "grey-box" models take into account the physical insight that an engineer would have about the structure they are modelling and have shown promising results in the structural engineering field among many others. This work compares black and grey-box models through a "green" lens, comparing them in terms of their environmental impact, and investigating how the high extrapolative performance of grey-box models can reduce their runtimes and therefore carbon emissions. The authors aim to develop physics-informed models with reduced computational costs, while maintaining high performance, illustrated through a structural health monitoring case study.

</details>


### [323] [Differential Subgroup Discovery: Characterizing Where Two Populations Differ, and Why](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.27741)
*Sascha Xu,Jilles Vreeken*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces DiffSub, a gradient-based method to identify differential subgroups—portions of feature space where two populations differ significantly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify and understand regions in a feature space where population-level differences are most pronounced, aiding in clinical, diagnostic, and treatment-effect analyses.
